rows.pali: 51.Idāni yaṃ vuttaṃ "samādhibhāvanāya ananurūpaṃ vihāraṃ pahāya anurūpe vihāre viharantenā"ti ettha yassa tāvācariyena saddhiṃ ekavihāre vasato phāsu hoti, tena tattheva kammaṭṭhānaṃ parisodhentena vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: 1. Now, it was said earlier: After that he should avoid a monastery unfavourable to the development of concentration and go to live in one that is favourable (III.28). In the first place one who finds it convenient to live with the teacher in the same monastery can live there while he is making certain of the meditation subject.

rows.pali: Sace tattha phāsu na hoti, yo añño gāvute vā aḍḍhayojane vā yojanamattepi vā sappāyo vihāro hoti, tattha vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: If it is inconvenient there, he can live in another monastery— a suitable one—a quarter or a half or even a whole league distant.

rows.pali: Evañhi sati kammaṭṭhānassa kismiñcideva ṭhāne sandehe vā satisammose vā jāte kālasseva vihāre vattaṃ katvā antarāmagge piṇḍāya caritvā bhattakiccapariyosāneyeva ācariyassa vasanaṭṭhānaṃ gantvā taṃdivasamācariyassa santike kammaṭṭhānaṃ sodhetvā dutiyadivase ācariyaṃ vanditvā nikkhamitvā antarāmagge piṇḍāya caritvā akilamantoyeva attano vasanaṭṭhānaṃ āgantuṃ sakkhissati.
rows.english: In that case, when he finds he is in doubt about, or has forgotten, some passage in the meditation subject, then he should do the duties in the monastery in good time and set out afterwards, going for alms on the way and arriving at the teacher’s dwelling place after his meal. He should make certain about the meditation subject that day in the teacher’s presence. Next day, after paying homage to the teacher, he should go for alms on his way back and so he can return to his own dwelling place without fatigue.

rows.pali: Yo pana yojanappamāṇepi phāsukaṭṭhānaṃ na labhati, tena kammaṭṭhāne sabbaṃ gaṇṭhiṭṭhānaṃ chinditvā suvisuddhaṃ āvajjanapaṭibaddhaṃ kammaṭṭhānaṃ katvā dūrampi gantvā samādhibhāvanāya ananurūpaṃ vihāraṃ pahāya anurūpe vihāre vihātabbaṃ.
rows.english: But one who finds no convenient place within even a league should clarify all difficulties about the meditation subject and make quite sure it has been properly attended to. Then he can even go far away and, avoiding a monastery unfavourable to development of concentration, live in one that is favourable.

rows.pali: 52.Tattha ananurūpo nāma aṭṭhārasannaṃ dosānaṃ aññatarena samannāgato.
rows.english: 2.Herein, one that is unfavourable has anyone of eighteen faults.

rows.pali: Tatrime aṭṭhārasa dosā – mahattaṃ, navattaṃ, jiṇṇattaṃ, panthanissitattaṃ, soṇḍī, paṇṇaṃ, pupphaṃ, phalaṃ, patthanīyatā, nagarasannissitatā, dārusannissitatā, khettasannissitatā, visabhāgānaṃ puggalānaṃ atthitā, paṭṭanasannissitatā, paccantasannissitatā, rajjasīmasannissitatā, asappāyatā, kalyāṇamittānaṃ alābhoti imesaṃ aṭṭhārasannaṃ dosānaṃ aññatarena dosena samannāgato ananurūpo nāma.
rows.english: These are: (1) largeness, (2) newness, (3) dilapidatedness, (4) a nearby road, (5) a pond, (6) [edible] leaves, (7) flowers, (8) fruits, (9) famousness, (10) a nearby city, (11) nearby timber trees, (12) nearby arable fields, (13) presence of incompatible persons, (14) a nearby port of entry, (15) nearness to the border countries, (16) nearness to the frontier of a kingdom, (17) unsuitability, (18) lack of good friends. [119] One with any of these faults is not favourable.

rows.pali: Na tattha vihātabbaṃ.
rows.english: He should not live there.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Mahāvihāre tāva bahū nānāchandā sannipatanti, te aññamaññaṃ paṭiviruddhatāya vattaṃ na karonti.
rows.english: 3.1.Firstly, people with varying aims collect in a large monastery. They conflict with each other and so neglect the duties.

rows.pali: Bodhiyaṅgaṇādīni asammaṭṭhāneva honti.
rows.english: The Enlightenment-tree terrace, etc., remain unswept,

rows.pali: Anupaṭṭhāpitaṃ pānīyaṃ paribhojanīyaṃ.
rows.english: the water for drinking and washing is not set out.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ gocaragāme piṇḍāya carissāmīti pattacīvaramādāya nikkhanto sace passati vattaṃ vā akataṃ pānīyaghaṭaṃ vā rittaṃ, athānena vattaṃ kātabbaṃ hoti, pānīyaṃ upaṭṭhāpetabbaṃ.
rows.english: So if he thinks, “I shall go to the alms-resort village for alms” and takes his bowl and robe and sets out, perhaps he sees that the duties have not been done or that a drinking-water pot is empty, and so the duty has to be done by him unexpectedly. Drinking water must be maintained.

rows.pali: Akaronto vattabhede dukkaṭaṃ āpajjati.
rows.english: By not doing it he would commit a wrongdoing in the breach of a duty.

rows.pali: Karontassa kālo atikkamati, atidivā paviṭṭho niṭṭhitāya bhikkhāya kiñci na labhati.
rows.english: But if he does it, he loses time. He arrives too late at the village and gets nothing because the alms giving is finished.

rows.pali: Paṭisallānagatopi sāmaṇeradaharabhikkhūnaṃ uccāsaddena saṅghakammehi ca vikkhipati.
rows.english: Also, when he goes into retreat, he is distracted by the loud noises of novices and young bhikkhus, and by acts of the Community [being carried out].

rows.pali: Yattha pana sabbaṃ vattaṃ katameva hoti, avasesāpi ca saṅghaṭṭanā natthi. Evarūpe mahāvihārepi vihātabbaṃ.
rows.english: However, he can live in a large monastery where all the duties are done and where there are none of the other disturbances.

rows.pali: Navavihāre bahu navakammaṃ hoti, akarontaṃ ujjhāyanti.
rows.english: 4. 2. In a new monastery there is much new building activity. People criticize someone who takes no part in it.

rows.pali: Yattha pana bhikkhū evaṃ vadanti "āyasmā yathāsukhaṃ samaṇadhammaṃ karotu, mayaṃ navakammaṃ karissāmā"ti evarūpe vihātabbaṃ.
rows.english: But he can live in such a monastery where the bhikkhus say, “Let the venerable one do the ascetic’s duties as much as he likes. We shall see to the building work. ”

rows.pali: Jiṇṇavihāre pana bahu paṭijaggitabbaṃ hoti, antamaso attano senāsanamattampi appaṭijaggantaṃ ujjhāyanti, paṭijaggantassa kammaṭṭhānaṃ parihāyati.
rows.english: 5.3. In a dilapidated monastery there is much that needs repair. People criticize someone who does not see about the repairing of at least his own lodging. When he sees to the repairs, his meditation subject suffers.

rows.pali: Panthanissite mahāpathavihāre rattindivaṃ āgantukā sannipatanti.
rows.english: 6.4. In a monastery with a nearby road, by a main street, visitors keep arriving night and day.

rows.pali: Vikāle āgatānaṃ attano senāsanaṃ datvā rukkhamūle vā pāsāṇapiṭṭhe vā vasitabbaṃ hoti.
rows.english: He has to give up his own lodging to those who come late, and he has to go and live at the root of a tree or on top of a rock.

rows.pali: Punadivasepi evamevāti kammaṭṭhānassa okāso na hoti.
rows.english: And next day it is the same. So there is no opportunity [to practice] his meditation subject.

rows.pali: Yattha pana evarūpo āgantukasambādho na hoti, tattha vihātabbaṃ.
rows.english: But he can live in one where there is no such disturbance by visitors.

rows.pali: Soṇḍī nāma pāsāṇapokkharaṇī hoti, tattha pānīyatthaṃ mahājano samosarati, nagaravāsīnaṃ rājakulūpakattherānaṃ antevāsikā rajanakammatthāya āgacchanti, tesaṃ bhājanadārudoṇikādīni pucchantānaṃ asuke ca asuke ca ṭhāneti dassetabbāni honti, evaṃ sabbakālampi niccabyāvaṭo hoti.
rows.english: 7.5. A pond is a rock pool. Numbers of people come there for drinking water. Pupils of city-dwelling elders supported by the royal family come to do dyeing work. When they ask for vessels, wood, tubs, etc., they must be shown where these things are. So he is kept all the time on the alert.

rows.pali: Yattha nānāvidhaṃ sākapaṇṇaṃ hoti, tatthassa kammaṭṭhānaṃ gahetvā divāvihāraṃ nisinnassāpi santike sākahārikā gāyamānā paṇṇaṃ uccinantiyo visabhāgasaddasaṅghaṭṭanena kammaṭṭhānantarāyaṃ karonti.
rows.english: 8.6. If he goes with his meditation subject to sit by day where there are many sorts of edible leaves, then women vegetable-gatherers, singing as they pick leaves nearby, endanger his meditation subject by disturbing it with sounds of the opposite sex.

rows.pali: Yattha pana nānāvidhā mālāgacchā supupphitā honti, tatrāpi tādisoyeva upaddavo.
rows.english: 7. And where there are many sorts of flowering shrubs in bloom there is the same danger too.

rows.pali: Yattha nānāvidhaṃ ambajambupanasādiphalaṃ hoti, tattha phalatthikā āgantvā yācanti, adentassa kujjhanti, balakkārena vā gaṇhanti, sāyanhasamaye vihāramajjhe caṅkamantena te disvā "kiṃ upāsakā evaṃ karothā"ti vuttā yathāruci akkosanti.
rows.english: 9.8. Where there are many sorts of fruits such as mangoes, rose-apples and jak-fruits, people who want fruits come and ask for them, and they get angry if he does not give them any, or they take them by force. When walking in the monastery in the evening he sees them and asks, “Why do you do so, lay followers? ” they abuse him as they please

rows.pali: Avāsāyapissa parakkamanti.
rows.english: and even try to evict him.

rows.pali: Patthanīye pana leṇasammate dakkhiṇagirihatthikucchicetiyagiricittalapabbatasadise vihāre viharantaṃ ayamarahāti sambhāvetvā vanditukāmā manussā samantā osaranti, tenassa na phāsu hoti, yassa pana taṃ sappāyaṃ hoti, tena divā aññatra gantvā rattiṃ vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: 10. 9. When he lives in a monastery that is famous and renowned in the world, like Dakkhiṇagiri1 Hatthikucchi, Cetiyagiri or Cittalapabbata, there are always people coming who want to pay homage to him, supposing that he is an Arahant, which inconveniences him. But if it suits him, he can live there at night and go elsewhere by day.

rows.pali: Nagarasannissite visabhāgārammaṇāni āpāthamāgacchanti, kumbhadāsiyopi ghaṭehi nighaṃsantiyo gacchanti, okkamitvā maggaṃ na denti, issaramanussāpi vihāramajjhe sāṇiṃ parikkhipitvā nisīdanti.
rows.english: 11.10. In one with a nearby city objects of the opposite sex come into focus. Women-pot carriers go by bumping into him with their jars and giving no room to pass. Also important people spread out carpets in the middle of the monastery and sit down.

rows.pali: Dārusannissaye pana yattha kaṭṭhāni ca dabbupakaraṇarukkhā ca santi, tattha kaṭṭhahārikā pubbe vuttasākapupphahārikā viya aphāsuṃ karonti, vihāre rukkhā santi, te chinditvā gharāni karissāmāti manussā āgantvā chindanti.
rows.english: 12. 11. One with nearby timber trees where there are timber trees and osiers useful for making framework is inconvenient because of the wood-gatherers there, like the gatherers of branches and fruits already mentioned.

rows.pali: Sace sāyanhasamayaṃ padhānagharā nikkhamitvā vihāramajjhe caṅkamanto te disvā "kiṃ upāsakā evaṃ karothā"ti vadati, yathāruci akkosanti, avāsāyapissa parakkamanti.
rows.english: If there are trees in a monastery, people come and cut them down to build houses with. When he has come out of his meditation room in the evening and is walking up and down in the monastery, if he sees them and asks, “Why do you do so, lay followers? ” they abuse him as they please and even try to evict him.

rows.pali: Yo pana khettasannissito hoti samantā khettehi parivārito, tattha manussā vihāramajjheyeva khalaṃ katvā dhaññaṃ maddanti, pamukhesu sayanti, aññampi bahuṃ aphāsuṃ karonti.
rows.english: 13.12. People make use of one with nearby arable fields, quite surrounded by fields. They make a threshing floor in the middle of the monastery itself. They thresh corn there, dry it in the forecourts,2 and cause great inconvenience.

rows.pali: Yatrāpi mahāsaṅghabhogo hoti, ārāmikā kulānaṃ gāvo rundhanti, udakavāraṃ paṭisedhenti, manussā vīhisīsaṃ gahetvā "passatha tumhākaṃ ārāmikānaṃ kamma"nti saṅghassa dassenti.
rows.english: And where there is extensive property belonging to the Community, the monastery attendants impound cattle belonging to families and deny the water supply [to their crops]. Then people bring an ear of paddy and show it to the Community saying “Look at your monastery attendants’ work.”

rows.pali: Tena tena kāraṇena rājarājamahāmattānaṃ gharadvāraṃ gantabbaṃ hoti, ayampi khettasannissiteneva saṅgahito.
rows.english: For one reason or another he has to go to the portals of the king or the king’s ministers. This [matter of property belonging to the Community] is included by [a monastery that is] near arable fields.

rows.pali: Visabhāgānaṃ puggalānaṃ atthitāti yattha aññamaññaṃ visabhāgaverī bhikkhū viharanti, ye kalahaṃ karontā mā, bhante, evaṃ karothāti vāriyamānā etassa paṃsukūlikassa āgatakālato paṭṭhāya naṭṭhāmhāti vattāro bhavanti.
rows.english: 14. 13. Presence of incompatible persons: where there are bhikkhus living who are incompatible and mutually hostile, when they clash and it is protested, “Venerable sirs, do not do so,” they exclaim, “We no longer count now that this refuse-rag wearer has come. ”

rows.pali: Yopi udakapaṭṭanaṃ vā thalapaṭṭanaṃ vā nissito hoti, tattha abhiṇhaṃ nāvāhi ca satthehi ca āgatamanussā okāsaṃ detha, pānīyaṃ detha, loṇaṃ dethāti ghaṭṭayantā aphāsuṃ karonti.
rows.english: 15. 14. One with a nearby water port of entry or land port of entry3 is made inconvenient by people constantly arriving respectively by ship or by caravan and crowding round, asking for space or for drinking water or salt.

rows.pali: Paccantasannissite pana manussā buddhādīsu appasannā honti.
rows.english: 16.15. In the case of one near the border countries, people have no trust in the Buddha, etc., there.

rows.pali: Rajjasīmasannissite rājabhayaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 16. In one near the frontier of a kingdom there is fear of kings.

rows.pali: Tañhi padesaṃ eko rājā na mayhaṃ vase vattatīti paharati, itaropi na mayhaṃ vase vattatīti.
rows.english: For perhaps one king attacks that place, thinking, “It does not submit to my rule,” and the other does likewise, thinking, “It does not submit to my rule.”

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ bhikkhu kadāci imassa rañño vijite vicarati, kadāci etassa.
rows.english: A bhikkhu lives there when it is conquered by one king and when it is conquered by the other.

rows.pali: Atha naṃ "carapuriso aya"nti maññamānā anayabyasanaṃ pāpenti.
rows.english: Then they suspect him of spying, and they bring about his undoing.

rows.pali: Asappāyatāti visabhāgarūpādiārammaṇasamosaraṇena vā amanussapariggahitatāya vā asappāyatā.
rows.english: 17.17. Unsuitability is that due to the risk of encountering visible data, etc., of the opposite sex as objects or to haunting by non-human beings.

rows.pali: Tatridaṃ vatthu.
rows.english: Here is a story.

rows.pali: Eko kira thero araññe vasati.
rows.english: An elder lived in a forest, it seems.

rows.pali: Athassa ekā yakkhinī paṇṇasāladvāre ṭhatvā gāyi.
rows.english: Then an ogress stood in the door of his leaf hut and sang.

rows.pali: So nikkhamitvā dvāre aṭṭhāsi, sā gantvā caṅkamanasīse gāyi.
rows.english: The elder came out and stood in the door. She went to the end of the walk and sang.

rows.pali: Thero caṅkamanasīsaṃ agamāsi.
rows.english: The elder went to the end of the walk.

rows.pali: Sā sataporise papāte ṭhatvā gāyi.
rows.english: She stood in a chasm a hundred fathoms deep and sang.

rows.pali: Thero paṭinivatti.
rows.english: The elder recoiled.

rows.pali: Atha naṃ sā vegenāgantvā gahetvā "mayā, bhante, na eko na dve tumhādisā khāditā"ti āha.
rows.english: Then she suddenly grabbed him saying, “Venerable sir, it is not just one or two of the likes of you I have eaten. ”

rows.pali: Kalyāṇamittānaṃ alābhoti yattha na sakkā hoti ācariyaṃ vā ācariyasamaṃ vā upajjhāyaṃ vā upajjhāyasamaṃ vā kalyāṇamittaṃ laddhuṃ.
rows.english: 18.18. Lack of good friends: where it is not possible to find a good friend as a teacher or the equivalent of a teacher or a preceptor or the equivalent of a preceptor,

rows.pali: Tattha so kalyāṇamittānaṃ alābho mahādosoyevāti imesaṃ aṭṭhārasannaṃ dosānaṃ aññatarena samannāgato ananurūpoti veditabbo.
rows.english: the lack of good friends there is a serious fault. One that has any of those eighteen faults should be understood as unfavourable.

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ aṭṭhakathāsu –
rows.english: And this is said in the commentaries:

rows.pali: 53.Yo pana gocaragāmato nātidūranāccāsannatādīhi pañcahaṅgehi samannāgato, ayaṃ anurūpo nāma.
rows.english: 19.One that has the five factors beginning with “not too far from and not too near to” the alms resort is called favourable.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā – "kathañca, bhikkhave, senāsanaṃ pañcaṅgasamannāgataṃ hoti?
rows.english: For this is said by the Blessed One: “And how has a lodging five factors, bhikkhus?

rows.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, senāsanaṃ nātidūraṃ hoti nāccāsannaṃ gamanāgamanasampannaṃ, divā appākiṇṇaṃ rattiṃ appasaddaṃ appanigghosaṃ, appaḍaṃsamakasavātātapasarīsapasamphassaṃ, tasmiṃ kho pana senāsane viharantassa appakasireneva uppajjanti cīvarapiṇḍapātasenāsanagilānapaccayabhesajjaparikkhārā.
rows.english: Here, bhikkhus, (1) a lodging is not too far, not too near, and has a path for going and coming. (2) It is little frequented by day with little sound and few voices by night. (3) There is little contact with gadflies, flies, wind, burning [sun] and creeping things. (4) One who lives in that lodging easily obtains robes, alms food, lodging, and the requisite of medicine as cure for the sick.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ kho pana senāsane therā bhikkhū viharanti bahussutā āgatāgamā dhammadharā vinayadharā mātikādharā, te kālena kālaṃ upasaṅkamitvā paripucchati paripañhati 'idaṃ, bhante, kathaṃ imassa ko attho'ti, tassa te āyasmanto avivaṭañceva vivaranti, anuttānīkatañca uttānīkaronti, anekavihitesu ca kaṅkhaṭṭhāniyesu dhammesu kaṅkhaṃ paṭivinodenti.
rows.english: (5) In that lodging there are elder bhikkhus living who are learned, versed in the scriptures, observers of the Dhamma, observers of the Vinaya, observers of the Codes, and when from time to time one asks them questions, ‘How is this, venerable sir? What is the meaning of this? ’ then those venerable ones reveal the unrevealed, explain the unexplained, and remove doubt about the many things that raise doubts.

rows.pali: Evaṃ kho, bhikkhave, senāsanaṃ pañcaṅgasamannāgataṃ hotī"ti (a. ni. 10.11).
rows.english: This, bhikkhus, is how a lodging has five factors”(A V 15).

rows.pali: Ayaṃ "samādhibhāvanāya ananurūpaṃ vihāraṃ pahāya anurūpe vihāre viharantenā"ti ettha vitthāro.
rows.english: These are the details for the clause, “After that he should avoid a monastery unfavourable to the development of concentration and go to live in one that is favourable” (III.28).

rows.pali: 54.Khuddakapalibodhupacchedaṃ katvāti evaṃ patirūpe vihāre viharantena yepissa te honti khuddakapalibodhā, tepi upacchinditabbā.
rows.english: 20. Then he should sever the lesser impediments (III.28): one living in such a favourable monastery should sever any minor impediments that he may still have,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, dīghāni kesanakhalomāni chinditabbāni.
rows.english: that is to say, long head hair, nails, and body hair should be cut,

rows.pali: Jiṇṇacīvaresu daḷhīkammaṃ vā tunnakammaṃ vā kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: mending and patching of old robes should be done,

rows.pali: Kiliṭṭhāni vā rajitabbāni.
rows.english: or those that are soiled should be dyed.

rows.pali: Sace patte malaṃ hoti, patto pacitabbo.
rows.english: If there is a stain on the bowl, the bowl should be baked.

rows.pali: Mañcapīṭhādīni sodhetabbānīti.
rows.english: The bed, chair, etc., should be cleaned up.

rows.pali: "Ayaṃ khuddakapalibodhupacchedaṃ katvā"ti ettha vitthāro.
rows.english: These are the details for the clause, “Then he should sever the lesser impediments. ”

rows.pali: 55.Idāni sabbaṃ bhāvanāvidhānaṃ aparihāpentena bhāvetabboti ettha ayaṃ pathavīkasiṇaṃ ādiṃ katvā sabbakammaṭṭhānavasena vitthārakathā hoti.
rows.english: 21. Now, with the clause, And not overlook any of the directions for development (III.28), the time has come for the detailed exposition of all meditation subjects, starting with the earth kasiṇa.

rows.pali: Evaṃ upacchinnakhuddakapalibodhena hi bhikkhunā pacchābhattaṃ piṇḍapātapaṭikkantena bhattasammadaṃ paṭivinodetvā pavivitte okāse sukhanisinnena katāya vā akatāya vā pathaviyā nimittaṃ gaṇhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: [THE EARTH KASIṆA] When a bhikkhu has thus severed the lesser impediments, then, on his return from his alms round after his meal and after he has got rid of drowsiness due to the meal, he should sit down comfortably in a secluded place and apprehend the sign in earth that is either made up or not made up.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ –
rows.english: 22. For this is said:4

rows.pali: Tattha yena atītabhavepi sāsane vā isipabbajjāya vā pabbajitvā pathavīkasiṇe catukkapañcakajjhānāni nibbattitapubbāni, evarūpassa puññavato upanissayasampannassa akatāya pathaviyā kasitaṭṭhāne vā khalamaṇḍale vā nimittaṃ uppajjati, mallakattherassa viya.
rows.english: 23. Herein, when in a previous becoming a man has gone forth into homelessness in the Dispensation or [outside it] with the rishis’ going forth and has already produced the jhāna tetrad or pentad on the earth kasiṇa, and so has such merit and the support [of past practice of jhāna] as well, then the sign arises in him on earth that is not made up, that is to say, on a ploughed area or on a threshing floor, as in the Elder Mallaka’s case.

rows.pali: Tassa kirāyasmato kasitaṭṭhānaṃ olokentassa taṃṭhānappamāṇameva nimittaṃ udapādi.
rows.english: It seems that while that venerable one was looking at a ploughed area the sign arose in him the size of that area.

rows.pali: So taṃ vaḍḍhetvā pañcakajjhānāni nibbattetvā jhānapadaṭṭhānaṃ vipassanaṃ paṭṭhapetvā arahattaṃ pāpuṇi.
rows.english: He extended it and attained the jhāna pentad. Then by establishing insight with the jhāna as the basis for it, he reached Arahantship.

rows.pali: Yo panevaṃ akatādhikāro hoti, tena ācariyasantike uggahitakammaṭṭhānavidhānaṃ avirādhetvā cattāro kasiṇadose pariharantena kasiṇaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: [MAKING AN EARTH KASIṆA] 24. But when a man has had no such previous practice, he should make a kasiṇa, guarding against the four faults of a kasiṇa and not overlooking any of the directions for the meditation subject learnt from the teacher.

rows.pali: Nīlapītalohitaodātasambhedavasena hi cattāro pathavīkasiṇadosā.
rows.english: Now, the four faults of the earth kasiṇa are due to the intrusion of blue, yellow, red or white.

rows.pali: Tasmā nīlādivaṇṇaṃ mattikaṃ aggahetvā gaṅgāvahe mattikāsadisāya aruṇavaṇṇāya mattikāya kasiṇaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: So instead of using clay of such colours, he should make the kasiṇa of clay like that in the stream of the Gangā,5 which is the colour of the dawn.

rows.pali: Tañca kho vihāramajjhe sāmaṇerādīnaṃ sañcaraṇaṭṭhāne na kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: And he should make it not in the middle of the monastery in a place where novices, etc., are about

rows.pali: Vihārapaccante pana paṭicchannaṭṭhāne pabbhāre vā paṇṇasālāya vā saṃhārimaṃ vā tatraṭṭhakaṃ vā kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: but on the confines of the monastery in a screened place, either under an overhanging rock or in a leaf hut. He can make it either portable or as a fixture.

rows.pali: Tatra saṃhārimaṃ catūsu daṇḍakesu pilotikaṃ vā cammaṃ vā kaṭasārakaṃ vā bandhitvā tattha apanītatiṇamūlasakkharakathalikāya sumadditāya mattikāya vuttappamāṇaṃ vaṭṭaṃ limpetvā kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: 25.Of these, a portable one should be made by tying rags of leather or matting onto four sticks and smearing thereon a disk of the size already mentioned, using clay picked clean of grass, roots, gravel, and sand, and well kneaded.

rows.pali: Taṃ parikammakāle bhūmiyaṃ attharitvā oloketabbaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of the preliminary work it should be laid on the ground and looked at.

rows.pali: Tatraṭṭhakaṃ bhūmiyaṃ padumakaṇṇikākārena khāṇuke ākoṭetvā vallīhi vinandhitvā kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: A fixture should be made by knocking stakes into the ground in the form of a lotus calyx, lacing them over with creepers.

rows.pali: Yadi sā mattikā nappahoti, adho aññaṃ pakkhipitvā uparibhāge suparisodhitāya aruṇavaṇṇāya mattikāya vidatthicaturaṅgulavitthāraṃ vaṭṭaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: If the clay is insufficient, then other clay should be put underneath and a disk a span and four fingers across made on top of that with the quite pure dawn-coloured clay.

rows.pali: Etadeva hi pamāṇaṃ sandhāya "suppamattaṃ vā sarāvamattaṃ vā"ti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: For it was with reference only to measurement that it was said above either the size of a bushel or the size of a saucer (§22).

rows.pali: "Sāntake no anantake"tiādi panassa paricchedatthāya vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But that is bounded, not unbounded was said to show its delimitedness.

rows.pali: 56.Tasmā evaṃ vuttappamāṇaparicchedaṃ katvā rukkhapāṇikā visabhāgavaṇṇaṃ samuṭṭhapeti.
rows.english: 26. So, having thus made it delimited and of the size prescribed, he should scrape it down with a stone trowel—a wooden trowel turns it a bad colour,

rows.pali: Tasmā taṃ aggahetvā pāsāṇapāṇikāya ghaṃsetvā samaṃ bherītalasadisaṃ katvā taṃ ṭhānaṃ sammajjitvā nhatvā āgantvā kasiṇamaṇḍalato aḍḍhateyyahatthantare padese paññatte vidatthicaturaṅgulapādake suatthate pīṭhe nisīditabbaṃ.
rows.english: so that should not be employed—and make it as even as the surface of a drum. Then he should sweep the place out and have a bath. On his return he should seat himself on a well-covered chair with legs a span and four fingers high, prepared in a place that is two and a half cubits [that is, two and a half times elbow to finger-tip] from the kasiṇa disk.

rows.pali: Tato dūratare nisinnassa hi kasiṇaṃ na upaṭṭhāti, āsannatare kasiṇadosā paññāyanti.
rows.english: For the kasiṇa does not appear plainly to him if he sits further off than that; and if he sits nearer than that, faults in the kasiṇa appear.

rows.pali: Uccatare nisinnena gīvaṃ onamitvā oloketabbaṃ hoti, nīcatare jaṇṇukāni rujanti.
rows.english: If he sits higher up, he has to look at it with his neck bent; and if he sits lower down, his knees ache.

rows.pali: Tasmā vuttanayeneva nisīditvā "appassādā kāmā"tiādinā nayena kāmesu ādīnavaṃ paccavekkhitvā kāmanissaraṇe sabbadukkhasamatikkamupāyabhūte nekkhamme jātābhilāsena buddhadhammasaṅghaguṇānussaraṇena pītipāmojjaṃ janayitvā "ayaṃ dāni sā sabbabuddha paccekabuddha ariyasāvakehi paṭipannā nekkhammapaṭipadā"ti paṭipattiyā sañjātagāravena "addhā imāya paṭipadāya pavivekasukharasassa bhāgī bhavissāmī"ti ussāhaṃ janayitvā samena ākārena cakkhūni ummīletvā nimittaṃ gaṇhantena bhāvetabbaṃ.
rows.english: [STARTING CONTEMPLATION] 27.So, after seating himself in the way stated, he should review the dangers in sense desires in the way beginning, “Sense desires give little enjoyment” (M I 91) and arouse longing for the escape from sense desires, for the renunciation that is the means to the surmounting of all suffering. He should next arouse joy of happiness by recollecting the special qualities of the Buddha, the Dhamma, and the Sangha; then awe by thinking, “Now, this is the way of renunciation entered upon by all Buddhas, Paccekabuddhas and noble disciples”; and then eagerness by thinking, “In this way I shall surely come to know the taste of the bliss of seclusion.” [125] After that he should open his eyes moderately, apprehend the sign, and so proceed to develop it. 6

rows.pali: Atiummīlayato hi cakkhu kilamati, maṇḍalañca ativibhūtaṃ hoti, tenassa nimittaṃ nuppajjati.
rows.english: 28.If he opens his eyes too wide, they get fatigued and the disk becomes too obvious, which prevents the sign becoming apparent to him.

rows.pali: Atimandaṃ ummīlayato maṇḍalamavibhūtaṃ hoti, cittañca līnaṃ hoti, evampi nimittaṃ nuppajjati.
rows.english: If he opens them too little, the disk is not obvious enough, and his mind becomes drowsy, which also prevents the sign becoming apparent to him.

rows.pali: Tasmā ādāsatale mukhanimittadassinā viya samenākārena cakkhūni ummīletvā nimittaṃ gaṇhantena bhāvetabbaṃ, na vaṇṇo paccavekkhitabbo, na lakkhaṇaṃ manasikātabbaṃ.
rows.english: So he should develop it by apprehending the sign (nimitta), keeping his eyes open moderately, as if he were seeing the reflection of his face (mukha-nimitta) on the surface of a looking- glass. 7 29.The colour should not be reviewed. The characteristic should not be given attention.8

rows.pali: Apica vaṇṇaṃ amuñcitvā nissayasavaṇṇaṃ katvā ussadavasena paṇṇattidhamme cittaṃ paṭṭhapetvā manasi kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: But rather, while not ignoring the colour, attention should be given by setting the mind on the [name] concept as the most outstanding mental datum, relegating the colour to the position of a property of its physical support.

rows.pali: Pathavī mahī, medinī, bhūmi, vasudhā, vasundharātiādīsu pathavīnāmesu yamicchati, yadassa saññānukūlaṃ hoti, taṃ vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: That [conceptual state] can be called by anyone he likes among the names for earth (pathavī) such as “earth” (pathavī), “the Great One” (mahī), “the Friendly One” (medinī), “ground” (bhūmi), “the Provider of Wealth” (vasudhā), “the Bearer of Wealth” (vasudharā), etc., whichever suits his manner of perception.

rows.pali: Apica pathavīti etadeva nāmaṃ pākaṭaṃ, tasmā pākaṭavaseneva pathavī pathavīti bhāvetabbaṃ.
rows.english: Still “earth” is also a name that is obvious, so it can be developed with the obvious one by saying “earth, earth.”

rows.pali: Kālena ummīletvā kālena nimīletvā āvajjitabbaṃ.
rows.english: It should be adverted to now with eyes open, now with eyes shut.

rows.pali: Yāva uggahanimittaṃ nuppajjati, tāva kālasatampi kālasahassampi tato bhiyyopi eteneva nayena bhāvetabbaṃ.
rows.english: And he should go on developing it in this way a hundred times, a thousand times, and even more than that, until the learning sign arises.

rows.pali: 57.Tassevaṃ bhāvayato yadā nimīletvā āvajjantassa ummīlitakāle viya āpāthamāgacchati, tadā uggahanimittaṃ jātaṃ nāma hoti.
rows.english: 30. When, while he is developing it in this way, it comes into focus9 as he adverts with his eyes shut exactly as it does with his eyes open, then the learning sign is said to have been produced.

rows.pali: Tassa jātakālato paṭṭhāya na tasmiṃ ṭhāne nisīditabbaṃ.
rows.english: After its production he should no longer sit in that place;10

rows.pali: Attano vasanaṭṭhānaṃ pavisitvā tattha nisinnena bhāvetabbaṃ.
rows.english: he should return to his own quarters and go on developing it sitting there.

rows.pali: Pādadhovanapapañcaparihāratthaṃ panassa ekapaṭalikupāhanā ca kattaradaṇḍo ca icchitabbo.
rows.english: But in order to avoid the delay of foot washing, a pair of single- soled sandals and a walking stick are desirable.

rows.pali: Athānena sace taruṇo samādhi kenacideva asappāyakāraṇena nassati, upāhanā āruyha kattaradaṇḍaṃ gahetvā taṃ ṭhānaṃ gantvā nimittaṃ ādāya āgantvā sukhanisinnena bhāvetabbaṃ, punappunaṃ samannāharitabbaṃ, takkāhataṃ vitakkāhataṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: Then if the new concentration vanishes through some unsuitable encounter, he can put his sandals on, take his walking stick, and go back to the place to re-apprehend the sign there. When he returns he should seat himself comfortably and develop it by reiterated reaction to it and by striking at it with thought and applied thought.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ karontassa anukkamena nīvaraṇāni vikkhambhanti, kilesā sannisīdanti, upacārasamādhinā cittaṃ samādhiyati, paṭibhāganimittaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: [THE COUNTERPART SIGN] 31. As he does so, the hindrances eventually become suppressed, the defilements subside, the mind becomes concentrated with access concentration, and the counterpart sign arises.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ purimassa ca uggahanimittassa imassa ca viseso, uggahanimitte kasiṇadoso paññāyati, paṭibhāganimittaṃ thavikato nihatādāsamaṇḍalaṃ viya sudhotasaṅkhathālaṃ viya valāhakantarā nikkhantacandamaṇḍalaṃ viya meghamukhe balākā viya uggahanimittaṃ padāletvā nikkhantamiva tato sataguṇaṃ sahassaguṇaṃ suparisuddhaṃ hutvā upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: The difference between the earlier learning sign and the counterpart sign is this. In the learning sign any fault in the kasiṇa is apparent. But the counterpart sign [126] appears as if breaking out from the learning sign, and a hundred times, a thousand times more purified, like a looking-glass disk drawn from its case, like a mother-of-pearl dish well washed, like the moon’s disk coming out from behind a cloud, like cranes against a thunder cloud.

rows.pali: Tañca kho neva vaṇṇavantaṃ, na saṇṭhānavantaṃ.
rows.english: But it has neither colour nor shape;

rows.pali: Yadi hi taṃ īdisaṃ bhaveyya, cakkhuviññeyyaṃ siyā oḷārikaṃ sammasanupagaṃ tilakkhaṇabbhāhataṃ, na panetaṃ tādisaṃ.
rows.english: for if it had, it would be cognizable by the eye, gross, susceptible of comprehension [by insight—(see XX.2f.)] and stamped with the three characteristics. 11 But it is not like that.

rows.pali: Kevalañhi samādhilābhino upaṭṭhānākāramattaṃ saññajametanti.
rows.english: For it is born only of perception in one who has obtained concentration, being a mere mode of appearance.12

rows.pali: 58.Uppannakālato ca panassa paṭṭhāya nīvaraṇāni vikkhambhitāneva honti, kilesā sannisinnāva, upacārasamādhinā cittaṃ samāhitamevāti.
rows.english: But as soon as it arises the hindrances are quite suppressed, the defilements subside, and the mind becomes concentrated in access concentration.

rows.pali: Duvidho hi samādhi upacārasamādhi ca appanāsamādhi ca.
rows.english: [THE TWO KINDS OF CONCENTRATION] 32.Now, concentration is of two kinds, that is to say, access concentration and absorption concentration:

rows.pali: Dvīhākārehi cittaṃ samādhiyati upacārabhūmiyaṃ vā paṭilābhabhūmiyaṃ vā.
rows.english: the mind becomes concentrated in two ways, that is, on the plane of access and on the plane of obtainment.

rows.pali: Tattha upacārabhūmiyaṃ nīvaraṇappahānena cittaṃ samāhitaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Herein, the mind becomes concentrated on the plane of access by the abandonment of the hindrances,

rows.pali: Paṭilābhabhūmiyaṃ aṅgapātubhāvena.
rows.english: and on the plane of obtainment by the manifestation of the jhāna factors.

rows.pali: Dvinnaṃ pana samādhīnaṃ idaṃ nānākāraṇaṃ, upacāre aṅgāni na thāmajātāni honti, aṅgānaṃ athāmajātattā, yathā nāma daharo kumārako ukkhipitvā ṭhapiyamāno punappunaṃ bhūmiyaṃ patati, evameva upacāre uppanne cittaṃ kālena nimittamārammaṇaṃ karoti, kālena bhavaṅgamotarati.
rows.english: 33.The difference between the two kinds of concentration is this. The factors are not strong in access. It is because they are not strong that when access has arisen, the mind now makes the sign its object and now re-enters the life- continuum,13 just as when a young child is lifted up and stood on its feet, it repeatedly falls down on the ground.

rows.pali: Appanāyaṃ pana aṅgāni thāmajātāni honti, tesaṃ thāmajātattā, yathā nāma balavā puriso āsanā vuṭṭhāya divasampi tiṭṭheyya, evameva appanāsamādhimhi uppanne cittaṃ sakiṃ bhavaṅgavāraṃ chinditvā kevalampi rattiṃ kevalampi divasaṃ tiṭṭhati, kusalajavanapaṭipāṭivaseneva pavattatīti.
rows.english: But the factors are strong in absorption. It is because they are strong that when absorption concentration has arisen, the mind, having once interrupted the flow of the life-continuum, carries on with a stream of profitable impulsion for a whole night and for a whole day, just as a healthy man, after rising from his seat, could stand for a whole day.

rows.pali: Tatra yadetaṃ upacārasamādhinā saddhiṃ paṭibhāganimittaṃ uppannaṃ, tassa uppādanaṃ nāma atidukkaraṃ.
rows.english: 34. The arousing of the counterpart sign, which arises together with access concentration, is very difficult.

rows.pali: Tasmā sace teneva pallaṅkena taṃ nimittaṃ vaḍḍhetvā appanaṃ adhigantuṃ sakkoti, sundaraṃ.
rows.english: Therefore if he is able to arrive at absorption in that same session by extending the sign, it is good.

rows.pali: No ce sakkoti, athānena taṃ nimittaṃ appamattena cakkavattigabbho viya rakkhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: If not, then he must guard the sign diligently as if it were the foetus of a Wheel-turning Monarch (World-ruler).

rows.pali: Evañhi –
59.Tatrāyaṃ rakkhaṇavidhi –
rows.english: 35.Herein, the way of guarding it is this:

rows.pali: Tatrassa yasmiṃ āvāse vasantassa anuppannaṃ vā nimittaṃ nuppajjati, uppannaṃ vā vinassati, anupaṭṭhitā ca sati na upaṭṭhāti, asamāhitañca cittaṃ na samādhiyati, ayaṃ asappāyo.
rows.english: 36.1. Herein, an abode is unsuitable if, while he lives in it, the unarisen sign does not arise in him or is lost when it arises, and where unestablished mindfulness fails to become established and the unconcentrated mind fails to become concentrated.

rows.pali: Yattha nimittaṃ uppajjati ceva thāvarañca hoti, sati upaṭṭhāti, cittaṃ samādhiyati nāgapabbatavāsīpadhāniyatissattherassa viya, ayaṃ sappāyo.
rows.english: That is suitable in which the sign arises and becomes confirmed, in which mindfulness becomes established and the mind becomes concentrated, as in the Elder Padhāniya-Tissa, resident at Nāgapabbata.

rows.pali: Tasmā yasmiṃ vihāre bahū āvāsā honti, tattha ekamekasmiṃ tīṇi tīṇi divasāni vasitvā yatthassa cittaṃ ekaggaṃ hoti, tattha vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: So if a monastery has many abodes he can try them one by one, living in each for three days, and stay on where his mind becomes unified.

rows.pali: Āvāsasappāyatāya hi tambapaṇṇidīpamhi cūḷanāgaleṇe vasantā tattheva kammaṭṭhānaṃ gahetvā pañcasatā bhikkhū arahattaṃ pāpuṇiṃsu.
rows.english: For it was due to suitability of abode that five hundred bhikkhus reached Arahantship while still dwelling in the Lesser Nāga Cave (Cūḷa-nāga-leṇa) in Tambapaṇṇi Island (Sri Lanka) after apprehending their meditation subject there.

rows.pali: Sotāpannādīnaṃ pana aññattha ariyabhūmiṃ patvā tattha arahattappattānañca gaṇanā natthi.
rows.english: There is no counting the stream- enterers who have reached Arahantship there after reaching the noble plane elsewhere;

rows.pali: Evamaññesupi cittalapabbatavihārādīsu.
rows.english: so too in the monastery of Cittalapabbata, and others.

rows.pali: Gocaragāmo pana yo senāsanato uttarena vā dakkhiṇena vā nātidūre diyaḍḍhakosabbhantare hoti sulabhasampannabhikkho, so sappāyo.
rows.english: 37. 2. An alms-resort village lying to the north or south of the lodging, not too far, within one kosa and a half, and where alms food is easily obtained, is suitable.

rows.pali: Viparīto asappāyo.
rows.english: The opposite kind is unsuitable. 14

rows.pali: Bhassanti dvattiṃsatiracchānakathāpariyāpannaṃ asappāyaṃ, tañhissa nimittantaradhānāya saṃvattati.
rows.english: 38.3. Speech: that included in the thirty-two kinds of aimless talk is unsuitable; for it leads to the disappearance of the sign.

rows.pali: Dasakathāvatthunissitaṃ sappāyaṃ, tampi mattāya bhāsitabbaṃ.
rows.english: But talk based on the ten examples of talk is suitable, though even that should be discussed with moderation. 15

rows.pali: Puggalopi atiracchānakathiko sīlādiguṇasampanno, yaṃ nissāya asamāhitaṃ vā cittaṃ samādhiyati, samāhitaṃ vā cittaṃ thirataraṃ hoti, evarūpo sappāyo.
rows.english: 39. 4. Person: one not given to aimless talk, who has the special qualities of virtue, etc., by acquaintanceship with whom the unconcentrated mind becomes concentrated, or the concentrated mind becomes more so, is suitable.

rows.pali: Kāyadaḷhībahulo pana tiracchānakathiko asappāyo.
rows.english: One who is much concerned with his body,16 who is addicted to aimless talk, is unsuitable;

rows.pali: So hi taṃ kaddamodakamiva acchaṃ udakaṃ malīnameva karoti, tādisañca āgamma koṭapabbatavāsīdaharasseva samāpattipi nassati, pageva nimittaṃ.
rows.english: for he only creates disturbances, like muddy water added to clear water. And it was owing to one such as this that the attainments of the young bhikkhu who lived at Koṭapabbata vanished, not to mention the sign.

rows.pali: Bhojanaṃ pana kassaci madhuraṃ, kassaci ambilaṃ sappāyaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 40. 5. Food: Sweet food suits one, sour food another.

rows.pali: Utupi kassaci sīto, kassaci uṇho sappāyo hoti.
rows.english: 6. Climate: a cool climate suits one, a warm one another.

rows.pali: Tasmā yaṃ bhojanaṃ vā utuṃ vā sevantassa phāsu hoti, asamāhitaṃ vā cittaṃ samādhiyati, samāhitaṃ vā thirataraṃ hoti, taṃ bhojanaṃ so ca utu sappāyo.
rows.english: So when he finds that by using certain food or by living in a certain climate he is comfortable, or his unconcentrated mind becomes concentrated, or his concentrated mind becomes more so, then that food or that climate is suitable.

rows.pali: Itaraṃ bhojanaṃ itaro ca utu asappāyo.
rows.english: Any other food or climate is unsuitable.

rows.pali: Iriyāpathesupi kassaci caṅkamo sappāyo hoti, kassaci sayanaṭṭhānanisajjānaṃ aññataro.
rows.english: 41. 7. Postures: walking suits one; standing or sitting or lying down suits another.

rows.pali: Tasmā taṃ āvāsaṃ viya tīṇi divasāni upaparikkhitvā yasmiṃ iriyāpathe asamāhitaṃ vā cittaṃ samādhiyati, samāhitaṃ vā thirataraṃ hoti, so sappāyo.
rows.english: So he should try them, like the abode, for three days each, and that posture is suitable in which his unconcentrated mind becomes concentrated or his concentrated mind becomes more so.

rows.pali: Itaro asappāyoti veditabbo.
rows.english: Any other should be understood as unsuitable.

rows.pali: Iti imaṃ sattavidhaṃ asappāyaṃ vajjetvā sappāyaṃ sevitabbaṃ.
rows.english: So he should avoid the seven unsuitable kinds and cultivate the suitable.

rows.pali: Evaṃ paṭipannassa hi nimittāsevanabahulassa nacireneva kālena hoti kassaci appanā.
rows.english: For when he practices in this way, assiduously cultivating the sign, then, “he need not wait too long until absorption shall his wish fulfil. ”

rows.pali: 60.Yassa pana evampi paṭipajjato na hoti, tena dasavidhaṃ appanākosallaṃ sampādetabbaṃ.
rows.english: 42.However, if this does not happen while he is practicing in this way, then he should have recourse to the ten kinds of skill in absorption.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ nayo, dasāhākārehi appanākosallaṃ icchitabbaṃ, vatthuvisadakiriyato, indriyasamattapaṭipādanato, nimittakusalato, yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ paggahetabbaṃ tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ paggaṇhāti, yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ niggahetabbaṃ tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ niggaṇhāti, yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ sampahaṃsitabbaṃ tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ sampahaṃseti, yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ ajjhupekkhitabbaṃ tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ ajjhupekkhati, asamāhitapuggalaparivajjanato, samāhitapuggalasevanato, tadadhimuttatoti.
rows.english: Here is the method. Skill in absorption needs [to be dealt with in] ten aspects: (1) making the basis clean, (2) maintaining balanced faculties, (3) skill in the sign, (4) he exerts the mind on an occasion when it should be exerted, (5) he restrains the mind on an occasion when it should be restrained, (6) he encourages the mind on an occasion when it should be encouraged, (7) he looks on at the mind with equanimity when it should be looked on at with equanimity, (8) avoidance of unconcentrated persons, (9) cultivation of concentrated persons, (10) resoluteness upon that (concentration).

rows.pali: 61.Tattha vatthuvisadakiriyā nāma ajjhattikabāhirānaṃ vatthūnaṃ visadabhāvakaraṇaṃ.
rows.english: 43.1. Herein, making the basis clean is cleansing the internal and the external basis.

rows.pali: Yadā hissa kesanakhalomāni dīghāni honti, sarīraṃ vā sedamalaggahitaṃ, tadā ajjhattikavatthu avisadaṃ hoti aparisuddhaṃ.
rows.english: For when his head hair, nails and body hair are long, or when the body is soaked with sweat, then the internal basis is unclean and unpurified.

rows.pali: Yadā panassa cīvaraṃ jiṇṇaṃ kiliṭṭhaṃ duggandhaṃ hoti, senāsanaṃ vā uklāpaṃ hoti, tadā bāhiravatthu avisadaṃ hoti aparisuddhaṃ.
rows.english: But when an old dirty smelly robe is worn or when the lodging is dirty, then the external basis is unclean and unpurified.

rows.pali: Ajjhattikabāhire ca vatthumhi avisade uppannesu cittacetasikesu ñāṇampi aparisuddhaṃ hoti, aparisuddhāni dīpakapallikavaṭṭitelāni nissāya uppannadīpasikhāya obhāso viya.
rows.english: When the internal and external bases are unclean, then the knowledge in the consciousness and consciousness- concomitants that arise is unpurified, like the light of a lamp’s flame that arises with an unpurified lamp-bowl, wick and oil as its support;

rows.pali: Aparisuddhena ñāṇena saṅkhāre sammasato saṅkhārāpi avibhūtā honti, kammaṭṭhānamanuyuñjato kammaṭṭhānampi vuḍḍhiṃ viruḷhiṃ vepullaṃ na gacchati.
rows.english: formations do not become evident to one who tries to comprehend them with unpurified knowledge, and when he devotes himself to his meditation subject, it does not come to growth, increase and fulfilment.

rows.pali: Visade pana ajjhattikabāhire vatthumhi uppannesu cittacetasikesu ñāṇampi visadaṃ hoti parisuddhaṃ, parisuddhāni dīpakapallikavaṭṭitelāni nissāya uppannadīpasikhāya obhāso viya.
rows.english: 44.But when the internal and external bases are clean, then the knowledge in the consciousness and consciousness-concomitants that arise is clean and purified, like the light of a lamp’s flame that arises with a purified lamp bowl, wick and oil as its support;

rows.pali: Parisuddhena ca ñāṇena saṅkhāre sammasato saṅkhārāpi vibhūtā honti, kammaṭṭhānamanuyuñjato kammaṭṭhānampi vuḍḍhiṃ virūḷhiṃ vepullaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: formations become evident to one who tries to comprehend them with purified knowledge, and as he devotes himself to his meditation subject, it comes to growth, increase and fulfilment.

rows.pali: 62.Indriyasamattapaṭipādanaṃnāma saddhādīnaṃ indriyānaṃ samabhāvakaraṇaṃ.
rows.english: 45. 2. Maintaining balanced faculties is equalizing the [five] faculties of faith and the rest.

rows.pali: Sace hissa saddhindriyaṃ balavaṃ hoti itarāni mandāni, tato vīriyindriyaṃ paggahakiccaṃ, satindriyaṃ upaṭṭhānakiccaṃ, samādhindriyaṃ avikkhepakiccaṃ, paññindriyaṃ dassanakiccaṃ kātuṃ na sakkoti, tasmā taṃ dhammasabhāvapaccavekkhaṇena vā yathā vā manasikaroto balavaṃ jātaṃ, tathā amanasikārena hāpetabbaṃ.
rows.english: For if his faith faculty is strong and the others weak, then the energy faculty cannot perform its function of exerting, the mindfulness faculty its function of establishing, the concentration faculty its function of not distracting, and the understanding faculty its function of seeing. So in that case the faith faculty should be modified either by reviewing the individual essences of the states [concerned, that is, the objects of attention] or by not giving [them] attention in the way in which the faith faculty became too strong.

rows.pali: Vakkalittheravatthu cettha nidassanaṃ.
rows.english: And this is illustrated by the story of the Elder Vakkali (S III 119).

rows.pali: Sace pana vīriyindriyaṃ balavaṃ hoti, atha neva saddhindriyaṃ adhimokkhakiccaṃ kātuṃ sakkoti, na itarāni itarakiccabhedaṃ, tasmā taṃ passaddhādibhāvanāya hāpetabbaṃ.
rows.english: 46.Then if the energy faculty is too strong, the faith faculty cannot perform its function of resolving, nor can the rest of the faculties perform their several functions. So in that case the energy faculty should be modified by developing tranquillity, and so on.

rows.pali: Tatrāpi soṇattheravatthu dassetabbaṃ.
rows.english: And this should be illustrated by the story of the Elder Soṇa (Vin I 179–85; A III 374–76).

rows.pali: Evaṃ sesesupi ekassa balavabhāve sati itaresaṃ attano kiccesu asamatthatā veditabbā.
rows.english: So too with the rest; for it should be understood that when anyone of them is too strong the others cannot perform their several functions.

rows.pali: Visesato panettha saddhāpaññānaṃ samādhivīriyānañca samataṃ pasaṃsanti.
rows.english: 47. However, what is particularly recommended is balancing faith with understanding, and concentration with energy.

rows.pali: Balavasaddho hi mandapañño muddhappasanno hoti, avatthusmiṃ pasīdati.
rows.english: For one strong in faith and weak in understanding has confidence uncritically and groundlessly.

rows.pali: Balavapañño mandasaddho kerāṭikapakkhaṃ bhajati, bhesajjasamuṭṭhito viya rogo atekiccho hoti.
rows.english: One strong in understanding and weak in faith errs on the side of cunning and is as hard to cure as one sick of a disease caused by medicine.

rows.pali: Ubhinnaṃ samatāya vatthusmiṃyeva pasīdati.
rows.english: With the balancing of the two a man has confidence only when there are grounds for it.

rows.pali: Balavasamādhiṃ pana mandavīriyaṃ samādhissa kosajjapakkhattā kosajjaṃ abhibhavati.
rows.english: Then idleness overpowers one strong in concentration and weak in energy, since concentration favours idleness.

rows.pali: Balavavīriyaṃ mandasamādhiṃ vīriyassa uddhaccapakkhattā uddhaccaṃ abhibhavati.
rows.english: Agitation overpowers one strong in energy and weak in concentration, since energy favours agitation.

rows.pali: Samādhi pana vīriyena saṃyojito kosajje patituṃ na labhati.
rows.english: But concentration coupled with energy cannot lapse into idleness,

rows.pali: Vīriyaṃ samādhinā saṃyojitaṃ uddhacce patituṃ na labhati, tasmā tadubhayaṃ samaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: and energy coupled with concentration cannot lapse into agitation. So these two should be balanced;

rows.pali: Ubhayasamatāya hi appanā hoti.
rows.english: for absorption comes with the balancing of the two.

rows.pali: Apica samādhikammikassa balavatīpi saddhā vaṭṭati.
rows.english: 48. Again, [concentration and faith should be balanced]. One working on concentration needs strong faith,

rows.pali: Evaṃ saddahanto okappento appanaṃ pāpuṇissati.
rows.english: since it is with such faith and confidence that he reaches absorption.

rows.pali: Samādhipaññāsu pana samādhikammikassa ekaggatā balavatī vaṭṭati.
rows.english: Then there is [balancing of] concentration and understanding. One working on concentration needs strong unification,

rows.pali: Evañhi so appanaṃ pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: since that is how he reaches absorption;

rows.pali: Vipassanākammikassa paññā balavatī vaṭṭati.
rows.english: and one working on insight needs strong understanding,

rows.pali: Evañhi so lakkhaṇapaṭivedhaṃ pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: since that is how he reaches penetration of characteristics;

rows.pali: Ubhinnaṃ pana samatāyapi appanā hotiyeva.
rows.english: but with the balancing of the two he reaches absorption as well.

rows.pali: Sati pana sabbattha balavatī vaṭṭati.
rows.english: 49. Strong mindfulness, however, is needed in all instances;

rows.pali: Sati hi cittaṃ uddhaccapakkhikānaṃ saddhāvīriyapaññānaṃ vasena uddhaccapātato kosajjapakkhena ca samādhinā kosajjapātato rakkhati, tasmā sā loṇadhūpanaṃ viya sabbabyañjanesu, sabbakammikaamacco viya ca sabbarājakiccesu sabbattha icchitabbā.
rows.english: for mindfulness protects the mind from lapsing into agitation through faith, energy and understanding, which favour agitation, and from lapsing into idleness through concentration, which favours idleness. So it is as desirable in all instances as a seasoning of salt in all sauces, as a prime minister in all the king’s business.

rows.pali: Tenāha – "sati ca pana sabbatthikā vuttā bhagavatā.
rows.english: Hence it is said [in the commentaries (D-a 788, M-a I 292, etc)]: “And mindfulness has been called universal by the Blessed One.

rows.pali: Kiṃ kāraṇā?
rows.english: For what reason?

rows.pali: Cittañhi satipaṭisaraṇaṃ, ārakkhapaccupaṭṭhānā ca sati, na vinā satiyā cittassa paggahaniggaho hotī"ti.
rows.english: Because the mind has mindfulness as its refuge, and mindfulness is manifested as protection, and there is no exertion and restraint of the mind without mindfulness. ”

rows.pali: 63.Nimittakosallaṃ nāma pathavīkasiṇādikassa cittekaggatānimittassa akatassa karaṇakosallaṃ, katassa ca bhāvanākosallaṃ, bhāvanāya laddhassa rakkhaṇakosallañca, taṃ idha adhippetaṃ.
rows.english: 50. 3. Skill in the sign is skill in producing the as yet unproduced sign of unification of mind through the earth kasiṇa, etc.; and it is skill in developing [the sign] when produced, and skill in protecting [the sign] when obtained by development. The last is what is intended here.

rows.pali: 64.Kathañca yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ paggahetabbaṃ, tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ paggaṇhāti?
rows.english: 51. 4. How does he exert the mind on an occasion when it should be exerted?

rows.pali: Yadāssa atisithilavīriyatādīhi līnaṃ cittaṃ hoti, tadā passaddhisambojjhaṅgādayo tayo abhāvetvā dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgādayo bhāveti.
rows.english: When his mind is slack with over-laxness of energy, etc., then, instead of developing the three enlightenment factors beginning with tranquillity, he should develop those beginning with investigation-of-states.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā –
rows.english: For this is said by the Blessed One:

rows.pali: Ettha ca yathāsakamāhāravasena dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgādīnaṃ bhāvanā veditabbā.
rows.english: 52. And here the development of the investigation-of-states enlightenment factor, etc., should be understood as the nutriment for each one respectively,

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ –
rows.english: for this is said:

rows.pali: Tattha sabhāvasāmaññalakkhaṇapaṭivedhavasena pavattamanasikāro kusalādīsu yoniso manasikāro nāma.
rows.english: 53. Herein, wise attention given to the profitable, etc., is attention occurring in penetration of individual essences and of [the three] general characteristics.

rows.pali: Ārambhadhātuādīnaṃ uppādanavasena pavattamanasikāro ārambhadhātuādīsu yoniso manasikāro nāma.
rows.english: Wise attention given to the element of initiative, etc., is attention occurring in the arousing of the element of initiative, and so on.

rows.pali: Tattha ārambhadhātūti paṭhamavīriyaṃ vuccati.
rows.english: Herein, initial energy is called the element of initiative.

rows.pali: Nikkamadhātūti kosajjato nikkhantattā tato balavataraṃ.
rows.english: The element of launching is stronger than that because it launches out from idleness.

rows.pali: Parakkamadhātūti paraṃ paraṃ ṭhānaṃ akkamanato tatopi balavataraṃ.
rows.english: The element of persistence is still stronger than that because it goes on persisting in successive later stages.

rows.pali: Pītisambojjhaṅgaṭṭhāniyā dhammāti pana pītiyā eva etaṃ nāmaṃ.
rows.english: States productive of the happiness enlightenment factor is a name for happiness itself;

rows.pali: Tassāpi uppādakamanasikārova yoniso manasikāro nāma.
rows.english: and attention that arouses that is wise attention.

rows.pali: Apica satta dhammā dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya saṃvattanti paripucchakatā, vatthuvisadakiriyā, indriyasamattapaṭipādanā, duppaññapuggalaparivajjanā, paññavantapuggalasevanā, gambhīrañāṇacariyapaccavekkhaṇā, tadadhimuttatāti.
rows.english: 54.There are, besides, seven things that lead to the arising of the investigation- of-states enlightenment factor: (i) asking questions, (ii) making the basis clean, (iii) balancing the faculties, (iv) avoidance of persons without understanding, (v) cultivation of persons with understanding, (vi) reviewing the field for the exercise of profound knowledge, (vii) resoluteness upon that [investigation of states].

rows.pali: Ekādasadhammā vīriyasambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya saṃvattanti apāyādibhayapaccavekkhaṇatā, vīriyāyattalokiyalokuttaravisesādhigamānisaṃsadassitā, "buddhapaccekabuddhamahāsāvakehi gatamaggo mayā gantabbo, so ca na sakkā kusītena gantu"nti evaṃ gamanavīthipaccavekkhaṇatā, dāyakānaṃ mahapphalabhāvakaraṇena piṇḍāpacāyanatā, "vīriyārambhassa vaṇṇavādī me satthā, so ca anatikkamanīyasāsano amhākañca bahūpakāro paṭipattiyā ca pūjiyamāno pūjito hoti na itarathā"ti evaṃ satthu mahattapaccavekkhaṇatā, "saddhammasaṅkhātaṃ me mahādāyajjaṃ gahetabbaṃ, tañca na sakkā kusītena gahetu"nti evaṃ dāyajjamahattapaccavekkhaṇatā, ālokasaññāmanasikārairiyāpathaparivattanaabbhokāsasevanādīhi thinamiddhavinodanatā, kusītapuggalaparivajjanatā, āraddhavīriyapuggalasevanatā, sammappadhānapaccavekkhaṇatā, tadadhimuttatāti.
rows.english: 55. Eleven things lead to the arising of the energy enlightenment factor: (i) reviewing the fearfulness of the states of loss such as the hell realms, etc., (ii) seeing benefit in obtaining the mundane and supramundane distinctions dependent on energy, (iii) reviewing the course of the journey [to be travelled] thus: “The path taken by the Buddhas, Paccekabuddhas, and the great disciples has to be taken by me, and it cannot be taken by an idler,” (iv) being a credit to the alms food by producing great fruit for the givers, (v) reviewing the greatness of the Master thus: “My Master praises the energetic, and this unsurpassable Dispensation that is so helpful to us is honoured in the practice, not otherwise,” (vi) reviewing the greatness of the heritage thus: “It is the great heritage called the Good Dhamma that is to be acquired by me, and it cannot be acquired by an idler,” (vii) removing stiffness and torpor by attention to perception of light, change of postures, frequenting the open air, etc., (viii) avoidance of idle persons, (ix) cultivation of energetic persons, (x) reviewing the right endeavours, (xi) resoluteness upon that [energy].

rows.pali: Ekādasadhammā pītisambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya saṃvattanti buddhānussati, dhamma… saṅgha… sīla… cāga… devatānussati, upasamānussati, lūkhapuggalaparivajjanatā, siniddhapuggalasevanatā, pasādaniyasuttantapaccavekkhaṇatā, tadadhimuttatāti.
rows.english: 56. Eleven things lead to the arising of the happiness enlightenment factor: the recollections (i) of the Buddha, (ii) of the Dhamma, (iii) of the Sangha, (iv) of virtue, (v) of generosity, and (vi) of deities, (vii) the recollection of peace, [133] (viii) avoidance of rough persons, (ix) cultivation of refined persons, (x) reviewing encouraging discourses, (xi) resoluteness upon that [happiness].

rows.pali: Iti imehi ākārehi ete dhamme uppādento dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgādayo bhāveti nāma.
rows.english: So by arousing these things in these ways he develops the investigation-of- states enlightenment factor, and the others.

rows.pali: Evaṃ yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ paggahetabbaṃ, tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ paggaṇhāti.
rows.english: This is how he exerts the mind on an occasion when it should be exerted.

rows.pali: 65.Kathaṃ yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ niggahetabbaṃ, tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ niggaṇhāti?
rows.english: 57. 5. How does he restrain the mind on an occasion when it should be restrained?

rows.pali: Yadāssa accāraddhavīriyatādīhi uddhataṃ cittaṃ hoti, tadā dhammavicayasambojjhaṅgādayo tayo abhāvetvā passaddhisambojjhaṅgādayo bhāveti.
rows.english: When his mind is agitated through over-energeticness, etc., then, instead of developing the three enlightenment factors beginning with investigation-of- states, he should develop those beginning with tranquillity;

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā –
rows.english: for this is said by the Blessed One:

rows.pali: Etthāpi yathāsakaṃ āhāravasena passaddhisambojjhaṅgādīnaṃ bhāvanā veditabbā.
rows.english: 58. And here the development of the tranquillity enlightenment factor, etc., should be understood as the nutriment for each one respectively,

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā –
rows.english: for this is said:

rows.pali: Tattha yathāssa passaddhiādayo uppannapubbā, taṃ ākāraṃ sallakkhetvā tesaṃ uppādanavasena pavattamanasikārova tīsupi padesu yoniso manasikāro nāma.
rows.english: 59.Herein wise attention given to the three instances is attention occurring in arousing tranquillity, etc., by observing the way in which they arose in him earlier.

rows.pali: Samathanimittanti ca samathassevetamadhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: The sign of serenity is a term for serenity itself,

rows.pali: Avikkhepaṭṭhena ca tasseva abyagganimittanti.
rows.english: and non-diversion is a term for that too in the sense of non-distraction.

rows.pali: Apica satta dhammā passaddhisambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya saṃvattanti paṇītabhojanasevanatā, utusukhasevanatā, iriyāpathasukhasevanatā, majjhattapayogatā, sāraddhakāyapuggalaparivajjanatā, passaddhakāyapuggalasevanatā, tadadhimuttatāti.
rows.english: 60.There are, besides, seven things that lead to the arising of the tranquillity enlightenment factor: (i) using superior food, (ii) living in a good climate, (iii) maintaining a pleasant posture, (iv) keeping to the middle, (v) avoidance of violent persons, (vi) cultivation of persons tranquil in body, (vii) resoluteness upon that [tranquillity].

rows.pali: Ekādasa dhammā samādhisambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya saṃvattanti vatthuvisadatā, nimittakusalatā, indriyasamattapaṭipādanatā, samaye cittassa niggahaṇatā, samaye cittassa paggahaṇatā, nirassādassa cittassa saddhāsaṃvegavasena sampahaṃsanatā, sammāpavattassa ajjhupekkhanatā, asamāhitapuggalaparivajjanatā, samāhitapuggalasevanatā, jhānavimokkhapaccavekkhaṇatā, tadadhimuttatāti.
rows.english: 61.Eleven things lead to the arising of the concentration enlightenment factor: (i) making the basis clean, (ii) skill in the sign, (iii) balancing the faculties, (iv) restraining the mind on occasion, (v) exerting the mind on occasion, (vi) encouraging the listless mind by means of faith and a sense of urgency, (vii) looking on with equanimity at what is occurring rightly, (viii) avoidance of unconcentrated persons, (ix) cultivation of concentrated persons, (x) reviewing of the jhānas and liberations, (xi) resoluteness upon that [concentration].

rows.pali: Pañca dhammā upekkhāsambojjhaṅgassa uppādāya saṃvattanti sattamajjhattatā, saṅkhāramajjhattatā, sattasaṅkhārakelāyanapuggalaparivajjanatā, sattasaṅkhāramajjhattapuggalasevanatā, tadadhimuttatāti.
rows.english: 62.Five things lead to the arising of the equanimity enlightenment factor: (i) maintenance of neutrality towards living beings; (ii) maintenance of neutrality towards formations (inanimate things); (iii) avoidance of persons who show favouritism towards beings and formations; (iv) cultivation of persons who maintain neutrality towards beings and formations; (v) resoluteness upon that [equanimity].

rows.pali: Iti imehākārehi ete dhamme uppādento passaddhisambojjhaṅgādayo bhāveti nāma.
rows.english: So by arousing these things in these ways he develops the tranquillity enlightenment factor, as well as the others.

rows.pali: Evaṃ yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ niggahetabbaṃ tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ niggaṇhāti.
rows.english: This is how he restrains the mind on an occasion when it should be restrained.

rows.pali: 66.Kathaṃ yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ sampahaṃsitabbaṃ, tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ sampahaṃseti?
rows.english: 63. 6. How does he encourage the mind on an occasion when it should be encouraged?

rows.pali: Yadāssa paññāpayogamandatāya vā upasamasukhānadhigamena vā nirassādaṃ cittaṃ hoti, tadā naṃ aṭṭhasaṃvegavatthupaccavekkhaṇena saṃvejeti.
rows.english: When his mind is listless owing to sluggishness in the exercise of understanding or to failure to attain the bliss of peace, then he should stimulate it by reviewing the eight grounds for a sense of urgency.

rows.pali: Aṭṭha saṃvegavatthūni nāma jātijarābyādhimaraṇāni cattāri, apāyadukkhaṃ pañcamaṃ, atīte vaṭṭamūlakaṃ dukkhaṃ, anāgate vaṭṭamūlakaṃ dukkhaṃ, paccuppanne āhārapariyeṭṭhimūlakaṃ dukkhanti.
rows.english: These are the four, namely, birth, aging, sickness, and death, with the suffering of the states of loss as the fifth, and also the suffering in the past rooted in the round [of rebirths], the suffering in the future rooted in the round [of rebirths], and the suffering in the present rooted in the search for nutriment.

rows.pali: Buddhadhammasaṅghaguṇānussaraṇena cassa pasādaṃ janeti.
rows.english: And he creates confidence by recollecting the special qualities of the Buddha, the Dhamma, and the Sangha.

rows.pali: Evaṃ yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ sampahaṃsitabbaṃ, tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ sampahaṃseti.
rows.english: This is how he encourages the mind on an occasion when it should be encouraged.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ ajjhupekkhitabbaṃ, tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ ajjhupekkhati?
rows.english: 64. 7. How does he look on at the mind with equanimity on an occasion when it should be looked on at with equanimity?

rows.pali: Yadāssa evaṃ paṭipajjato alīnaṃ anuddhataṃ anirassādaṃ ārammaṇe samappavattaṃ samathavīthipaṭipannaṃ cittaṃ hoti, tadāssa paggahaniggahasampahaṃsanesu na byāpāraṃ āpajjati, sārathi viya samappavattesu assesu.
rows.english: When he is practicing in this way and his mind follows the road of serenity, occurs evenly on the object, and is unidle, unagitated and not listless, then he is not interested to exert or restrain or encourage it; he is like a charioteer when the horses are progressing evenly.

rows.pali: Evaṃ yasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ ajjhupekkhitabbaṃ, tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ ajjhupekkhati.
rows.english: This is how he looks on at the mind with equanimity on an occasion when it should be looked on at with equanimity.

rows.pali: Asamāhitapuggalaparivajjanatā nāma nekkhammapaṭipadaṃ anāruḷhapubbānaṃ anekakiccapasutānaṃ vikkhittahadayānaṃ puggalānaṃ ārakā pariccāgo.
rows.english: 65.8. Avoidance of unconcentrated persons is keeping far away from persons who have never trodden the way of renunciation, who are busy with many affairs, and whose hearts are distracted.

rows.pali: Samāhitapuggalasevanatā nāma nekkhammapaṭipadaṃ paṭipannānaṃ samādhilābhīnaṃ puggalānaṃ kālena kālaṃ upasaṅkamanaṃ.
rows.english: 9. Cultivation of concentrated persons is approaching periodically persons who have trodden the way of renunciation and obtained concentration.

rows.pali: Tadadhimuttatānāma samādhiadhimuttatā samādhigarusamādhininnasamādhipoṇasamādhipabbhāratāti attho.
rows.english: 10. Resoluteness upon that is the state of being resolute upon concentration; the meaning is, giving concentration importance, tending, leaning and inclining to concentration.

rows.pali: Evametaṃ dasavidhaṃ appanākosallaṃ sampādetabbaṃ.
rows.english: This is how the tenfold skill in concentration should be undertaken.

rows.pali: 68.Tatrāyamatthadīpanā – yathā hi acheko madhukaro asukasmiṃ rukkhe pupphaṃ pupphitanti ñatvā tikkhena vegena pakkhando taṃ atikkamitvā paṭinivattento khīṇe reṇumhi sampāpuṇāti.
rows.english: 67.Here is the explanation of the meaning. When a too clever bee learns that a flower on a tree is blooming, it sets out hurriedly, overshoots the mark, turns back, and arrives when the pollen is finished;

rows.pali: Aparo acheko mandena javena pakkhando khīṇeyeva sampāpuṇāti.
rows.english: and another, not clever enough bee, who sets out with too slow a speed, arrives when the pollen is finished too;

rows.pali: Cheko pana samena javena pakkhando sukhena puppharāsiṃ sampatvā yāvadicchakaṃ reṇuṃ ādāya madhuṃ sampādetvā madhurasamanubhavati.
rows.english: but a clever bee sets out with balanced speed, arrives with ease at the cluster of flowers, takes as much pollen as it pleases and enjoys the honey-dew.

rows.pali: Yathā ca sallakattaantevāsikesu udakathālagate uppalapatte satthakammaṃ sikkhantesu eko acheko vegena satthaṃ pātento uppalapattaṃ dvidhā vā chindati, udake vā paveseti.
rows.english: 68.Again, when a surgeon’s pupils are being trained in the use of the scalpel on a lotus leaf in a dish of water, one who is too clever applies the scalpel hurriedly and either cuts the lotus leaf in two or pushes it under the water,

rows.pali: Aparo acheko chijjanapavesanabhayā satthakena phusitumpi na visahati.
rows.english: and another who is not clever enough does not even dare to touch it with the scalpel for fear of cutting it in two or pushing it under;

rows.pali: Cheko pana samena payogena tattha satthapahāraṃ dassetvā pariyodātasippo hutvā tathārūpesu ṭhānesu kammaṃ katvā lābhaṃ labhati.
rows.english: but one who is clever shows the scalpel stroke on it by means of a balanced effort, and being good at his craft he is rewarded on such occasions.

rows.pali: Yathā ca yo catubyāmappamāṇaṃ makkaṭasuttamāharati, so cattāri sahassāni labhatīti raññā vutte eko achekapuriso vegena makkaṭasuttamākaḍḍhanto tahiṃ tahiṃ chindatiyeva.
rows.english: 69. Again when the king announces, “Anyone who can draw out a spider’s thread four fathoms long shall receive four thousand,” one man who is too clever breaks the spider’s thread here and there by pulling it hurriedly,

rows.pali: Aparo acheko chedanabhayā hatthena phusitumpi na visahati.
rows.english: and another who is not clever enough does not dare to touch it with his hand for fear of breaking it,

rows.pali: Cheko pana koṭito paṭṭhāya samena payogena daṇḍake vedhetvā āharitvā lābhaṃ labhati.
rows.english: but a clever man pulls it out starting from the end with a balanced effort, winds it on a stick, and so wins the prize.

rows.pali: Yathā ca acheko niyāmako balavavāte laṅkāraṃ pūrento nāvaṃ videsaṃ pakkhandāpeti.
rows.english: 70.Again, a too clever [137] skipper hoists full sails in a high wind and sends his ship adrift,

rows.pali: Aparo acheko mandavāte laṅkāraṃ oropento nāvaṃ tattheva ṭhapeti.
rows.english: and another, not clever enough skipper, lowers his sails in a light wind and remains where he is,

rows.pali: Cheko pana mandavāte laṅkāraṃ pūretvā balavavāte aḍḍhalaṅkāraṃ katvā sotthinā icchitaṭṭhānaṃ pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: but a clever skipper hoists full sails in a light wind, takes in half his sails in a high wind, and so arrives safely at his desired destination.

rows.pali: Yathā ca yo telena achaḍḍento nāḷiṃ pūreti, so lābhaṃ labhatīti ācariyena antevāsikānaṃ vutte eko acheko lābhaluddho vegena pūrento telaṃ chaḍḍeti.
rows.english: 71.Again, when a teacher says, “Anyone who fills the oil-tube without spilling any oil will win a prize,” one who is too clever fills it hurriedly out of greed for the prize, and he spills the oil,

rows.pali: Aparo acheko telachaḍḍanabhayā āsiñcitumpi na visahati.
rows.english: and another who is not clever enough does not dare to pour the oil at all for fear of spilling it,

rows.pali: Cheko pana samena payogena pūretvā lābhaṃ labhati.
rows.english: but one who is clever fills it with a balanced effort and wins the prize.

rows.pali: Evameva eko bhikkhu uppanne nimitte sīghameva appanaṃ pāpuṇissāmīti gāḷhaṃ vīriyaṃ karoti, tassa cittaṃ accāraddhavīriyattā uddhacce patati, so na sakkoti appanaṃ pāpuṇituṃ.
rows.english: 72.Just as in these five similes, so too when the sign arises, one bhikkhu forces his energy, thinking “I shall soon reach absorption.” Then his mind lapses into agitation because of his mind’s over-exerted energy and he is prevented from reaching absorption.

rows.pali: Eko accāraddhavīriyatāya dosaṃ disvā kiṃ dānime appanāyāti vīriyaṃ hāpeti, tassa cittaṃ atilīnavīriyattā kosajje patati, sopi na sakkoti appanaṃ pāpuṇituṃ.
rows.english: Another who sees the defect in over-exertion slacks off his energy, thinking, “What is absorption to me now?” Then his mind lapses into idleness because of his mind’s too lax energy and he too is prevented from reaching absorption.

rows.pali: Yo pana īsakampi līnaṃ līnabhāvato uddhataṃ uddhaccato mocetvā samena payogena nimittābhimukhaṃ pavatteti, so appanaṃ pāpuṇāti, tādisena bhavitabbaṃ.
rows.english: Yet another who frees his mind from idleness even when it is only slightly idle and from agitation when only slightly agitated, confronting the sign with balanced effort, reaches absorption. One should be like the last-named.

rows.pali: Imamatthaṃ sandhāya etaṃ vuttaṃ –
rows.english: 73.It was with reference to this meaning that it was said above:

rows.pali: 69.Iti evaṃ nimittābhimukhaṃ mānasaṃ paṭipādayato panassa idāni appanā ijjhissatīti bhavaṅgaṃ upacchinditvā pathavī pathavīti anuyogavasena upaṭṭhitaṃ tadeva pathavīkasiṇaṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā manodvārāvajjanamuppajjati.
rows.english: 74. So, while he is guiding his mind in this way, confronting the sign, [then knowing]: “Now absorption will succeed,” there arises in him mind-door adverting with that same earth kasiṇa as its object, interrupting the [occurrence of consciousness as] life-continuum, and evoked by the constant repeating of “earth, earth.”

rows.pali: Tato tasmiṃyevārammaṇe cattāri pañca vā javanāni javanti.
rows.english: After that, either four or five impulsions impel on that same object,

rows.pali: Tesu avasāne ekaṃ rūpāvacaraṃ, sesāni kāmāvacarāni.
rows.english: the last one of which is an impulsion of the fine-material sphere. The rest are of the sense sphere,

rows.pali: Pakaticittehi balavataravitakkavicārapītisukhacittekaggatāni yāni appanāya parikammattā parikammānītipi, yathā gāmādīnaṃ āsannapadeso gāmūpacāro nagarūpacāroti vuccati, evaṃ appanāya āsannattā samīpacārattā vā upacārānītipi, ito pubbe parikammānaṃ, upari appanāya ca anulomato anulomānītipi vuccanti.
rows.english: but they have stronger applied thought, sustained thought, happiness, bliss, and unification of mind than the normal ones. They are called “preliminary work” [consciousnesses] because they are the preliminary work for absorption; [138] and they are also called “access” [consciousnesses] because of their nearness to absorption because they happen in its neighbourhood, just as the words “village access” and “city access” are used for a place near to a village, etc.; and they are also called “conformity” [consciousnesses] because they conform to those that precede the “preliminary work” [consciousnesses] and to the absorption that follows.

rows.pali: Yañcettha sabbantimaṃ, taṃ parittagottābhibhavanato, mahaggatagottabhāvanato ca gotrabhūtipi vuccati.
rows.english: And the last of these is also called “change- of-lineage” because it transcends the limited [sense-sphere] lineage and brings into being the exalted [fine-material-sphere] lineage. 18

rows.pali: Agahitaggahaṇena panettha paṭhamaṃ parikammaṃ, dutiyaṃ upacāraṃ, tatiyaṃ anulomaṃ, catutthaṃ gotrabhu.
rows.english: 75. But omitting repetitions,19 then either the first is the “preliminary work,” the second “access,” the third “conformity,” and the fourth, “change-of-lineage,”

rows.pali: Paṭhamaṃ vā upacāraṃ, dutiyaṃ anulomaṃ, tatiyaṃ gotrabhu, catutthaṃ pañcamaṃ vā appanācittaṃ.
rows.english: or else the first is “access,” the second “conformity,” and the third “change-of- lineage.” Then either the fourth [in the latter case] or the fifth [in the former case] is the absorption consciousness.

rows.pali: Catutthameva hi pañcamaṃ vā appeti, tañca kho khippābhiññadandhābhiññavasena.
rows.english: For it is only either the fourth or the fifth that fixes in absorption. And that is according as there is swift or sluggish direct- knowledge. (cf. XXI.117)

rows.pali: Tato paraṃ javanaṃ patati.
rows.english: Beyond that, impulsion lapses

rows.pali: Bhavaṅgassa vāro hoti.
rows.english: and the life-continuum20 takes over.

rows.pali: Ābhidhammikagodattatthero pana "purimā purimā kusalā dhammā pacchimānaṃ pacchimānaṃ kusalānaṃ dhammānaṃ āsevanapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.12) imaṃ suttaṃ vatvā āsevanapaccayena pacchimo pacchimo dhammo balavā hoti, tasmā chaṭṭhepi sattamepi appanā hotīti āha, taṃ aṭṭhakathāsu "attano matimattaṃ therasseta"nti vatvā paṭikkhittaṃ.
rows.english: 76.But the Abhidhamma scholar, the Elder Godatta, quoted this text: “Preceding profitable states are a condition, as repetition condition, for succeeding profitable states” (Paṭṭh I 5). Adding, “It is owing to the repetition condition that each succeeding state is strong, so there is absorption also in the sixth and seventh. ” 77.That is rejected by the commentaries with the remark that it is merely that elder’s opinion,

rows.pali: Catutthapañcamesuyeva pana appanā hoti.
rows.english: adding that, “It is only either in the fourth or the fifth21 that there is absorption.

rows.pali: Parato javanaṃ patitaṃ nāma hoti, bhavaṅgassa āsannattāti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: Beyond that, impulsion lapses. It is said to do so because of nearness of the life-continuum.”

rows.pali: Tameva vicāretvā vuttattā na sakkā paṭikkhipituṃ.
rows.english: And that has been stated in this way after consideration, so it cannot be rejected.

rows.pali: Yathā hi puriso chinnapapātābhimukho dhāvanto ṭhātukāmopi pariyante pādaṃ katvā ṭhātuṃ na sakkoti papāte eva patati, evaṃ chaṭṭhe vā sattame vā appetuṃ na sakkoti, bhavaṅgassa āsannattā.
rows.english: For just as a man who is running towards a precipice and wants to stop cannot do so when he has his foot on the edge but falls over it, so there can be no fixing in absorption in the sixth or the seventh because of the nearness to the life-continuum.

rows.pali: Tasmā catutthapañcamesuyeva appanā hotīti veditabbā.
rows.english: That is why it should be understood that there is absorption only in the fourth or the fifth.

rows.pali: Sā ca pana ekacittakkhaṇikāyeva.
rows.english: 78. But that absorption is only of a single conscious moment.

rows.pali: Sattasu hi ṭhānesu addhānaparicchedo nāma natthi paṭhamappanāyaṃ, lokiyābhiññāsu, catūsu maggesu, maggānantaraphale, rūpārūpabhavesu bhavaṅgajjhāne, nirodhassa paccaye nevasaññānāsaññāyatane, nirodhā vuṭṭhahantassa phalasamāpattiyanti.
rows.english: For there are seven instances in which the normal extent22 [of the cognitive series] does not apply. They are in the cases of the first absorption, the mundane kinds of direct- knowledge, the four paths, fruition next after the path, life-continuum jhāna in the fine-material and immaterial kinds of becoming, the base consisting of neither perception nor non-perception as condition for cessation [of perception and feeling], and the fruition attainment in one emerging from cessation.

rows.pali: Ettha maggānantaraphalaṃ tiṇṇaṃ upari na hoti.
rows.english: Here the fruition next after the path does not exceed three [consciousnesses in number];

rows.pali: Nirodhassa paccayo nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṃ dvinnamupari na hoti.
rows.english: the [consciousnesses] of the base consisting of neither perception nor non- perception as condition for cessation do not exceed two [in number]; there is no measure of the [number of consciousnesses in the] life-continuum in the fine- material and immaterial [kinds of becoming].

rows.pali: Rūpārūpesu bhavaṅgassa parimāṇaṃ natthi, sesaṭṭhānesu ekameva cittanti.
rows.english: In the remaining instances [the number of consciousnesses is] one only.

rows.pali: Iti ekacittakkhaṇikāyeva appanā.
rows.english: So absorption is of a single consciousness moment.

rows.pali: Tato bhavaṅgapāto.
rows.english: After that, it lapses into the life-continuum.

rows.pali: Atha bhavaṅgaṃ vocchinditvā jhānapaccavekkhaṇatthāya āvajjanaṃ, tato jhānapaccavekkhaṇanti.
rows.english: Then the life-continuum is interrupted by adverting for the purpose of reviewing the jhāna, next to which comes the reviewing of the jhāna.

rows.pali: Ettāvatā ca panesa vivicceva kāmehi vivicca akusalehi dhammehi savitakkaṃ savicāraṃ vivekajaṃ pītisukhaṃ paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ upasampajja viharati (dha. sa. 160; dī. ni. 1.226).
rows.english: [THE FIRST JHĀNA]79. At this point, “Quite secluded from sense desires, secluded from unprofitable things he enters upon and dwells in the first jhāna, which is accompanied by applied and sustained thought with happiness and bliss born of seclusion” (Vibh 245),

rows.pali: Evamanena pañcaṅgavippahīnaṃ pañcaṅgasamannāgataṃ tividhakalyāṇaṃ dasalakkhaṇasampannaṃ paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ adhigataṃ hoti pathavīkasiṇaṃ.
rows.english: and so he has attained the first jhāna, which abandons five factors, possesses five factors, is good in three ways, possesses ten characteristics, and is of the earth kasiṇa.

rows.pali: 70.Tattha vivicceva kāmehīti kāmehi viviccitvā vinā hutvā apakkamitvā.
rows.english: 80. Herein, quite secluded from sense desires means having secluded himself from, having become without, having gone away from, sense desires.

rows.pali: Yo panāyamettha evakāro, so niyamatthoti veditabbo.
rows.english: Now, this word quite (eva) should be understood to have the meaning of absoluteness.

rows.pali: Yasmā ca niyamattho, tasmā tasmiṃ paṭhamajjhānaṃ upasampajja viharaṇasamaye avijjamānānampi kāmānaṃ tassa paṭhamajjhānassa paṭipakkhabhāvaṃ kāmapariccāgeneva cassa adhigamaṃ dīpeti.
