rows.english: 151.Once this has been obtained in this way, and he has mastery in the five ways already described, then on emerging from the now familiar second jhāna he can regard the flaws in it thus: “This attainment is threatened by the nearness of applied and sustained thought; ‘Whatever there is in it of happiness, of mental excitement, proclaims its grossness’ (D I 37), and its factors are weakened by the grossness of the happiness so expressed.” He can bring the third jhāna to mind as quieter and so end his attachment to the second jhāna and set about doing what is needed for attaining the third.

rows.pali: Athassa yadā dutiyajjhānato vuṭṭhāya satassa sampajānassa jhānaṅgāni paccavekkhato pīti oḷārikato upaṭṭhāti, sukhañceva ekaggatā ca santato upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: 152. When he has emerged from the second jhāna [159] happiness appears gross to him as he reviews the jhāna factors with mindfulness and full awareness, while bliss and unification appear peaceful.

rows.pali: Tadāssa oḷārikaṅgappahānāya santaaṅgapaṭilābhāya ca tadeva nimittaṃ "pathavī pathavī"ti punappunaṃ manasikaroto "idāni tatiyajjhānaṃ uppajjissatī"ti bhavaṅgaṃ upacchinditvā tadeva pathavīkasiṇaṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā manodvārāvajjanaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: Then as he brings that same sign to mind as “earth, earth” again and again with the purpose of abandoning the gross factor and obtaining the peaceful factors, [knowing] “now the third jhāna will arise,” there arises in him mind-door adverting with that same earth kasiṇa as its object, interrupting the life-continuum.

rows.pali: Tato tasmiṃyevārammaṇe cattāri pañca vā javanāni javanti, yesaṃ avasāne ekaṃ rūpāvacaraṃ tatiyajjhānikaṃ, sesāni vuttanayeneva kāmāvacarānīti.
rows.english: After that, either four or five impulsions impel on that same object, the last one of which is an impulsion of the fine-material sphere belonging to the third jhāna. The rest are of the kinds already stated (§74).

rows.pali: Ettāvatā ca panesa pītiyā ca virāgā upekkhako ca viharati sato ca sampajāno, sukhañca kāyena paṭisaṃvedeti, yaṃ taṃ ariyā ācikkhanti upekkhako satimā sukhavihārīti, tatiyaṃ jhānaṃ upasampajja viharatīti (dī. ni. 1.230; dha. sa. 163).
rows.english: [THE THIRD JHĀNA] 153. And at this point, “With the fading away of happiness as well he dwells in equanimity, and mindful and fully aware, he feels bliss with his body; he enters upon and dwells in the third jhāna, on account of which the Noble Ones announce: ‘He dwells in bliss who has equanimity and is mindful’ (Vibh 245),

rows.pali: Evamanena ekaṅgavippahīnaṃ duvaṅgasamannāgataṃ tividhakalyāṇaṃ dasalakkhaṇasampannaṃ tatiyaṃ jhānaṃ adhigataṃ hoti pathavīkasiṇaṃ.
rows.english: and so he has attained the third jhāna, which abandons one factor, possesses two factors, is good in three ways, possesses ten characteristics, and is of the earth kasiṇa.

rows.pali: 83.Tattha pītiyā ca virāgāti virāgo nāma vuttappakārāya pītiyā jigucchanaṃ vā samatikkamo vā.
rows.english: 154.Herein, with the fading away of happiness as well (pītiyā ca virāgā): fading away is distaste for, or surmounting of, happiness of the kind already described.

rows.pali: Ubhinnaṃ pana antarā casaddo sampiṇḍanattho, so vūpasamaṃ vā sampiṇḍeti vitakkavicārānaṃ vūpasamaṃ vā.
rows.english: But the words “as well” (ca) between the two [words pītiyā and virāgā] have the meaning of a conjunction;44 they conjoin [to them] either the word “stilling” or the expression “the stilling of applied and sustained thought” [in the description of the second jhāna].

rows.pali: Tattha yadā vūpasamameva sampiṇḍeti, tadā "pītiyā ca virāgā kiñca bhiyyo vūpasamā cā"ti evaṃ yojanā veditabbā.
rows.english: Herein, when taken as conjoining “stilling” the construction to be understood is “with the fading away and, what is more, with the stilling, of happiness.”

rows.pali: Imissā ca yojanāya virāgo jigucchanattho hoti, tasmā "pītiyā jigucchanā ca vūpasamā cā"ti ayamattho daṭṭhabbo.
rows.english: With this construction “fading away” has the meaning of distaste; so the meaning can be regarded as “with distaste for, and with the stilling of, happiness.”

rows.pali: Yadā pana vitakkavicāravūpasamaṃ sampiṇḍeti, tadā "pītiyā ca virāgā, kiñca bhiyyo vitakkavicārānañca vūpasamā"ti evaṃ yojanā veditabbā.
rows.english: But when taken as conjoining the words “stilling of applied and sustained thought,” then the construction to be understood is “with the fading of happiness and, further, with the stilling of applied and sustained thought.”

rows.pali: Imissā ca yojanāya virāgo samatikkamanattho hoti, tasmā "pītiyā ca samatikkamā vitakkavicārānañca vūpasamā"ti ayamattho daṭṭhabbo.
rows.english: With this construction “fading away” has the meaning of surmounting; so this meaning can be regarded as “with the surmounting of happiness and with the stilling of applied and sustained thought. ”

rows.pali: Kāmañcete vitakkavicārā dutiyajjhāneyeva vūpasantā, imassa pana jhānassa maggaparidīpanatthaṃ vaṇṇabhaṇanatthañcetaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 155. Of course, applied and sustained thought have already been stilled in the second jhāna, too. However, this is said in order to show the path to this third jhāna and in order to recommend it.

rows.pali: Vitakkavicārānañca vūpasamāti hi vutte idaṃ paññāyati, nūna vitakkavicāravūpasamo maggo imassa jhānassāti.
rows.english: For when “with the stilling of applied and sustained thought” is said, it is declared that the path to this jhāna is necessarily by the stilling of applied and sustained thought.

rows.pali: Yathā ca tatiye ariyamagge appahīnānampi sakkāyadiṭṭhādīnaṃ "pañcannaṃ orambhāgiyānaṃ saṃyojanānaṃ pahānā"ti (dī. ni. 1.373; ma. ni. 2.133; saṃ. ni. 5.184; a. ni. 3.88) evaṃ pahānaṃ vuccamānaṃ vaṇṇabhaṇanaṃ hoti, tadadhigamāya ussukkānaṃ ussāhajanakaṃ, evameva idha avūpasantānampi vitakkavicārānaṃ vūpasamo vuccamāno vaṇṇabhaṇanaṃ hoti.
rows.english: And just as, although mistaken view of individuality, etc., are not abandoned in the attaining of the third noble path [but in the first], yet when it is recommended by describing their abandonment thus, “With the abandoning of the five lower fetters” (A I 232), [160] then it awakens eagerness in those trying to attain that third noble path— so too, when the stilling of applied and sustained thought is mentioned, though they are not actually stilled here [but in the second], this is a recommendation.

rows.pali: Tenāyamattho vutto "pītiyā ca samatikkamā vitakkavicārānañca vūpasamā"ti.
rows.english: Hence the meaning expressed is this: “With the surmounting of happiness and with the stilling of applied and sustained thought. ”

rows.pali: 84.Upekkhako ca viharatīti ettha upapattito ikkhatīti upekkhā.
rows.english: 156. He dwells in equanimity: it watches [things] as they arise (UPApattito IKKHATI), thus it is equanimity (upekkhā—or onlooking);

rows.pali: Samaṃ passati, apakkhapatitā hutvā passatīti attho.
rows.english: it sees fairly, sees without partiality (a-pakkha-patita), is the meaning.

rows.pali: Tāya visadāya vipulāya thāmagatāya samannāgatattā tatiyajjhānasamaṅgī upekkhakoti vuccati.
rows.english: A possessor of the third jhāna is said to “dwell in equanimity” since he possesses equanimity that is clear, abundant and sound.

rows.pali: Upekkhā pana dasavidhā hoti chaḷaṅgupekkhā, brahmavihārupekkhā, bojjhaṅgupekkhā, vīriyupekkhā, saṅkhārupekkhā, vedanupekkhā, vipassanupekkhā, tatramajjhattupekkhā, jhānupekkhā, pārisuddhupekkhāti.
rows.english: Equanimity is of ten kinds; six-factored equanimity, equanimity as a divine abiding, equanimity as an enlightenment factor, equanimity of energy, equanimity about formations, equanimity as a feeling, equanimity about insight, equanimity as specific neutrality, equanimity of jhāna and equanimity of purification.

rows.pali: Tattha yā "idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu cakkhunā rūpaṃ disvā neva sumano hoti, na dummano, upekkhako ca viharati sato sampajāno"ti (a. ni. 6.1) evamāgatā khīṇāsavassa chasu dvāresu iṭṭhāniṭṭhachaḷārammaṇāpāthe parisuddhapakatibhāvāvijahanākārabhūtā upekkhā, ayaṃ chaḷaṅgupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 157. Herein, six factored equanimity is a name for the equanimity in one whose cankers are destroyed. It is the mode of non-abandonment of the natural state of purity when desirable or undesirable objects of the six kinds come into focus in the six doors described thus: “Here a bhikkhu whose cankers are destroyed is neither glad nor sad on seeing a visible object with the eye: he dwells in equanimity, mindful and fully aware” (A III 279).

rows.pali: Yā pana "upekkhāsahagatena cetasā ekaṃ disaṃ pharitvā viharatī"ti (dī. ni. 1.556; ma. ni. 1.77) evamāgatā sattesu majjhattākārabhūtā upekkhā, ayaṃ brahmavihārupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 158. Equanimity as a divine abiding is a name for equanimity consisting in the mode of neutrality towards beings described thus: “He dwells intent upon one quarter with his heart endued with equanimity” (D I 251).

rows.pali: Yā "upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṃ bhāveti vivekanissita"nti (ma. ni. 1.27) evamāgatā sahajātadhammānaṃ majjhattākārabhūtā upekkhā, ayaṃ bojjhaṅgupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 159. Equanimity as an enlightenment factor is a name for equanimity consisting in the mode of neutrality in conascent states described thus: “He develops the equanimity enlightenment factor depending on relinquishment” (M I 11).

rows.pali: Yā pana "kālenakālaṃ upekkhānimittaṃ manasikarotī"ti (a. ni. 3.103) evamāgatā anaccāraddhanātisithilavīriyasaṅkhātā upekkhā, ayaṃ vīriyupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 160. Equanimity of energy is a name for the equanimity otherwise known as neither over-strenuous nor over-lax energy described thus: “From time to time he brings to mind the sign of equanimity” (A I 257).

rows.pali: Yā "kati saṅkhārupekkhā samathavasena uppajjanti, kati saṅkhārupekkhā vipassanāvasena uppajjanti. Aṭṭha saṅkhārupekkhā samathavasena uppajjanti. Dasa saṅkhārupekkhā vipassanāvasena uppajjantī"ti (paṭi. ma. 1.57) evamāgatā nīvaraṇādipaṭisaṅkhāsantiṭṭhanā gahaṇe majjhattabhūtā upekkhā, ayaṃ saṅkhārupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 161. Equanimity about formations is a name for equanimity consisting in neutrality about apprehending reflexion and composure regarding the hindrances, etc., described thus: “How many kinds of equanimity about formations arise through concentration? How many kinds of equanimity about formations arise through insight? Eight kinds of equanimity about formations arise through concentration. Ten kinds of equanimity about formations arise through insight”45 (Paṭis I 64). [161]

rows.pali: Yā pana "yasmiṃ samaye kāmāvacaraṃ kusalaṃ cittaṃ uppannaṃ hoti upekkhāsahagata"nti (dha. sa. 150) evamāgatā adukkhamasukhasaññitā upekkhā, ayaṃ vedanupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 162. Equanimity as a feeling is a name for the equanimity known as neither- pain-nor-pleasure described thus: “On the occasion on which a sense-sphere profitable consciousness has arisen accompanied by equanimity” (Dhs §156).

rows.pali: Yā "yadatthi yaṃ bhūtaṃ, taṃ pajahati, upekkhaṃ paṭilabhatī"ti (ma. ni. 3.71; a. ni. 7.55) evamāgatā vicinane majjhattabhūtā upekkhā, ayaṃ vipassanupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 163. Equanimity about insight is a name for equanimity consisting in neutrality about investigation described thus: “What exists, what has become, that he abandons, and he obtains equanimity” (M II 264–65, A IV 70f).

rows.pali: Yā pana chandādīsu yevāpanakesu āgatā sahajātānaṃ samavāhitabhūtā upekkhā, ayaṃ tatramajjhattupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 164. Equanimity as specific neutrality is a name for equanimity consisting in the equal efficiency of conascent states; it is contained among the “or-whatever states” beginning with zeal (XIV.133; Dhs-a 132).

rows.pali: Yā "upekkhako ca viharatī"ti (dī. ni. 1.230; dha. sa. 163) evamāgatā aggasukhepi tasmiṃ apakkhapātajananī upekkhā, ayaṃ jhānupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 165. Equanimity of jhāna is a name for equanimity producing impartiality towards even the highest bliss described thus: “He dwells in equanimity” (Vibh 245).

rows.pali: Yā pana "upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṃ catutthaṃ jhāna"nti (dī. ni. 1.232; dha. sa. 165) evamāgatā sabbapaccanīkaparisuddhā paccanīkavūpasamanepi abyāpārabhūtā upekkhā, ayaṃ pārisuddhupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 166. Purifying equanimity is a name for equanimity purified of all opposition, and so consisting in uninterestedness in stilling opposition described thus: “The fourth jhāna, which … has mindfulness purified by equanimity” (Vibh 245).

rows.pali: Tatra chaḷaṅgupekkhā ca brahmavihārupekkhā ca bojjhaṅgupekkhā ca tatramajjhattupekkhā ca jhānupekkhā ca pārisuddhupekkhā ca atthato ekā, tatramajjhattupekkhāva hoti.
rows.english: 167. Herein, six-factored equanimity, equanimity as a divine abiding, equanimity as an enlightenment factor, equanimity as specific neutrality, equanimity of jhāna and purifying equanimity are one in meaning, that is, equanimity as specific neutrality.

rows.pali: Tena tena avatthābhedena panassā ayaṃ bhedo.
rows.english: Their difference, however, is one of position,46

rows.pali: Ekassāpi sato sattassa kumārayuvatherasenāpatirājādivasena bhedo viya.
rows.english: like the difference in a single being as a boy, a youth, an adult, a general, a king, and so on.

rows.pali: Tasmā tāsu yattha chaḷaṅgupekkhā, na tattha bojjhaṅgupekkhādayo.
rows.english: Therefore of these it should be understood that equanimity as an enlightenment factor, etc., are not found where there is six-factored equanimity;

rows.pali: Yattha vā pana bojjhaṅgupekkhā, na tattha chaḷaṅgupekkhādayo hontīti veditabbā.
rows.english: or that six-factored equanimity, etc., are not found where there is equanimity as an enlightenment factor.

rows.pali: Yathā cetāsamatthato ekībhāvo, evaṃ saṅkhārupekkhā vipassanupekkhānampi.
rows.english: And just as these have one meaning, so also equanimity about formations and equanimity about insight have one meaning too;

rows.pali: Paññā eva hi sā kiccavasena dvidhā bhinnā.
rows.english: for they are simply understanding classed in these two ways according to function.

rows.pali: Yathā hi purisassa sāyaṃ gehaṃ paviṭṭhaṃ sappaṃ ajapadadaṇḍaṃ gahetvā pariyesamānassa taṃ thusakoṭṭhake nipannaṃ disvā "sappo nu kho, no"ti avalokentassa sovattikattayaṃ disvā nibbematikassa "sappo, na sappo"ti vicinane majjhattatā hoti, evameva yā āraddhavipassakassa vipassanāñāṇena lakkhaṇattaye diṭṭhe saṅkhārānaṃ aniccabhāvādivicinane majjhattatā uppajjati, ayaṃ vipassanupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: 168. Just as, when a man has seen a snake go into his house in the evening and has hunted for it with a forked stick, and then when he has seen it lying in the grain store and has looked to discover whether it is actually a snake or not, and then by seeing three marks47 has no more doubt, and so there is neutrality in him about further investigating whether or not it is a snake, [162] so too, when a man has begun insight, and he sees with insight knowledge the three characteristics, then there is neutrality in him about further investigating the impermanence, etc., of formations, and that neutrality is called equanimity about insight.

rows.pali: Yathā pana tassa purisassa ajapadadaṇḍena gāḷhaṃ sappaṃ gahetvā "kiṃ tāhaṃ imaṃ sappaṃ aviheṭhento attānañca iminā aḍaṃsāpento muñceyya"nti muñcanākārameva pariyesato gahaṇe majjhattatā hoti.
rows.english: 169. But just as, when the man has caught hold of the snake securely with the forked stick and thinks, “How shall I get rid of the snake without hurting it or getting bitten by it?” then as he is seeking only the way to get rid of it, there is neutrality in him about the catching hold of it,

rows.pali: Evameva yā lakkhaṇattayassa diṭṭhattā āditte viya tayo bhave passato saṅkhāraggahaṇe majjhattatā, ayaṃ saṅkhārupekkhā nāma.
rows.english: so too, when a man, through seeking the three characteristics, sees the three kinds of becoming as if burning, then there is neutrality in him about catching hold of formations, and that neutrality is called equanimity about formations.

rows.pali: Iti vipassanupekkhāya siddhāya saṅkhārupekkhāpi siddhāva hoti.
rows.english: 170. So when equanimity about insight is established, equanimity about formations is established too.

rows.pali: Iminā panesā vicinanaggahaṇesu majjhattasaṅkhātena kiccena dvidhā bhinnāti.
rows.english: But it is divided into two in this way according to function, in other words, according to neutrality about investigating and about catching hold.

rows.pali: Vīriyupekkhā pana vedanupekkhā ca aññamaññañca avasesāhi ca atthato bhinnā evāti.
rows.english: Equanimity of energy and equanimity as feeling are different both from each other and from the rest.

rows.pali: Iti imāsu upekkhāsu jhānupekkhā idhādhippetā.
rows.english: 171. So, of these kinds of equanimity, it is equanimity of jhāna that is intended here.

rows.pali: Sā majjhattalakkhaṇā, anābhogarasā, abyāpārapaccupaṭṭhānā, pītivirāgapadaṭṭhānāti.
rows.english: That has the characteristic of neutrality. Its function is to be unconcerned. It is manifested as uninterestedness. Its proximate cause is the fading away of happiness.

rows.pali: Etthāha, nanu cāyamatthato tatramajjhattupekkhāva hoti, sā ca paṭhamadutiyajjhānesupi atthi.
rows.english: Here it may be said: Is this not simply equanimity as specific neutrality in the meaning? And that exists in the first and second jhānas as well;

rows.pali: Tasmā tatrāpi upekkhako ca viharatīti evamayaṃ vattabbā siyā, sā kasmā na vuttāti.
rows.english: so this clause, “He dwells in equanimity,” ought to be stated of those also. Why is it not?

rows.pali: Aparibyattakiccato.
rows.english: —[It may be replied:] Because its function is unevident there

rows.pali: Aparibyattañhi tassā tattha kiccaṃ vitakkādīhi abhibhūtattā.
rows.english: since it is overshadowed by applied thought and the rest.

rows.pali: Idha panāyaṃ vitakkavicārapītīhi anabhibhūtattā ukkhittasirā viya hutvā paribyattakiccā jātā, tasmā vuttāti.
rows.english: But it appears here with a quite evident function, with head erect, as it were, because it is not overshadowed by applied thought and sustained thought and happiness. That is why it is stated here.

rows.pali: 85.Idāni sato ca sampajānoti ettha saratīti sato.
rows.english: 172. Now, as to mindful and fully aware: here, he remembers (sarati), thus he is mindful (sata).

rows.pali: Sampajānātīti sampajāno.
rows.english: He has full awareness (sampajānāti), thus he is fully aware (sampajāna).

rows.pali: Puggalena sati ca sampajaññañca vuttaṃ.
rows.english: This is mindfulness and full awareness stated as personal attributes.

rows.pali: Tattha saraṇalakkhaṇā sati, asammussanarasā, ārakkhapaccupaṭṭhānā.
rows.english: Herein, mindfulness has the characteristic of remembering. Its function is not to forget. It is manifested as guarding.

rows.pali: Asammohalakkhaṇaṃ sampajaññaṃ, tīraṇarasaṃ, pavicayapaccupaṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: Full awareness has the characteristic of non-confusion. Its function is to investigate (judge). It is manifested as scrutiny.

rows.pali: Tattha kiñcāpi idaṃ satisampajaññaṃ purimajjhānesupi atthi.
rows.english: 173. Herein, although this mindfulness and this full awareness exist in the earlier jhānas as well—

rows.pali: Muṭṭhasatissa hi asampajānassa upacāramattampi na sampajjati, pageva appanā.
rows.english: for one who is forgetful and not fully aware does not attain even access, let alone absorption—

rows.pali: Oḷārikattā pana tesaṃ jhānānaṃ bhūmiyaṃ viya purisassa cittassa gati sukhā hoti, abyattaṃ tattha satisampajaññakiccaṃ.
rows.english: yet, because of the [comparative] grossness of those jhānas, the mind’s going is easy [there], like that of a man on [level] ground, and so the functions of mindfulness and full awareness are not evident in them.

rows.pali: Oḷārikaṅgappahānena pana sukhumattā imassa jhānassa purisassa khuradhārāyaṃ viya satisampajaññakiccapariggahitā eva cittassa gati icchitabbāti idheva vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But it is only stated here because the subtlety of this jhāna, which is due to the abandoning of the gross factors, requires that the mind’s going always includes the functions of mindfulness and full awareness, like that of a man on a razor’s edge.

rows.pali: Kiñca bhiyyo, yathā dhenupago vaccho dhenuto apanīto arakkhiyamāno punadeva dhenuṃ upagacchati, evamidaṃ tatiyajjhānasukhaṃ pītito apanītaṃ, taṃ satisampajaññārakkhena arakkhiyamānaṃ punadeva pītiṃ upagaccheyya, pītisampayuttameva siyā.
rows.english: 174.What is more, just as a calf that follows a cow returns to the cow when taken away from her if not prevented, so too, when this third jhāna is led away from happiness, it would return to happiness if not prevented by mindfulness and full awareness, and would rejoin happiness.

rows.pali: Sukhe vāpi sattā sārajjanti, idañca atimadhuraṃ sukhaṃ, tato paraṃ sukhābhāvā.
rows.english: And besides, beings are greedy for bliss, and this kind of bliss is exceedingly sweet since there is none greater.

rows.pali: Satisampajaññānubhāvena panettha sukhe asārajjanā hoti, no aññathāti imampi atthavisesaṃ dassetuṃ idamidheva vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: But here there is non-greed for the bliss owing to the influence of the mindfulness and full awareness, not for any other reason. And so it should also be understood that it is stated only here in order to emphasize this meaning too.

rows.pali: Idāni sukhañca kāyena paṭisaṃvedetīti ettha kiñcāpi tatiyajjhānasamaṅgino sukhapaṭisaṃvedanābhogo natthi.
rows.english: 175. Now, as to the clause he feels bliss with his body: here, although in one actually possessed of the third jhāna there is no concern about feeling bliss,

rows.pali: Evaṃ santepi yasmā tassa nāmakāyena sampayuttaṃ sukhaṃ.
rows.english: nevertheless he would feel the bliss associated with his mental body,

rows.pali: Yaṃ vā taṃ nāmakāyasampayuttaṃ sukhaṃ, taṃsamuṭṭhānenassa yasmā atipaṇītena rūpena rūpakāyo phuṭo, yassa phuṭattā jhānā vuṭṭhitopi sukhaṃ paṭisaṃvedeyya.
rows.english: and after emerging from the jhāna he would also feel bliss since his material body would have been affected by the exceedingly superior matter originated by that bliss associated with the mental body. 48

rows.pali: Tasmā etamatthaṃ dassento sukhañca kāyena paṭisaṃvedetīti āha.
rows.english: It is in order to point to this meaning that the words “he feels bliss with his body” are said.

rows.pali: 86.Idāni yaṃ taṃ ariyā ācikkhanti upekkhako satimā sukhavihārīti ettha yaṃjhānahetu yaṃjhānakāraṇā taṃ tatiyajjhānasamaṅgipuggalaṃ buddhādayo ariyā ācikkhanti desenti paññapenti paṭṭhapenti vivaranti vibhajanti uttānīkaronti pakāsenti, pasaṃsantīti adhippāyo.
rows.english: 176. Now, as to the clause, that … on account of which the Noble Ones announce: He dwells in bliss who has equanimity and is mindful: here it is the jhāna, on account of which as cause, on account of which as reason, the Noble Ones, that is to say, the Enlightened Ones, etc., “announce, teach, declare, establish, reveal, expound, explain, clarify” (Vibh 259) that person who possesses the third jhāna—they praise, is what is intended.

rows.pali: Kinti?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Upekkhako satimā sukhavihārīti.
rows.english: Because “he dwells in bliss who has equanimity and is mindful.

rows.pali: Taṃ tatiyaṃ jhānaṃ upasampajja viharatīti evamettha yojanā veditabbā.
rows.english: He enters upon and dwells in that third jhāna” (taṃ … tatiyaṃ jhānaṃ upasampajja viharati) is how the construction should be understood here.

rows.pali: Kasmā pana taṃ te evaṃ pasaṃsantīti?
rows.english: But why do they praise him thus?

rows.pali: Pasaṃsārahato.
rows.english: Because he is worthy of praise.

rows.pali: Ayañhi yasmā atimadhurasukhe sukhapāramippattepi tatiyajjhāne upekkhako, na tattha sukhābhisaṅgena ākaḍḍhiyati.
rows.english: 177. For this man is worthy of praise since he has equanimity towards the third jhāna though it possesses exceedingly sweet bliss and has reached the perfection of bliss, and he is not drawn towards it by a liking for the bliss,

rows.pali: Yathā ca pīti na uppajjati, evaṃ upaṭṭhitasatitāya satimā. Yasmā ca ariyakantaṃ ariyajanasevitameva ca asaṃkiliṭṭhaṃ sukhaṃ nāmakāyena paṭisaṃvedeti, tasmā pasaṃsāraho hoti.
rows.english: and he is mindful with the mindfulness established in order to prevent the arising of happiness, and he feels with his mental body the undefiled bliss beloved of Noble Ones, cultivated by Noble Ones.

rows.pali: Iti pasaṃsārahato naṃ ariyā te evaṃ pasaṃsāhetubhūte guṇe pakāsento "upekkhako satimā sukhavihārī"ti evaṃ pasaṃsantīti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: Because he is worthy of praise in this way, it should be understood, Noble Ones praise him with the words, “He dwells in bliss who has equanimity and is mindful,” thus declaring the special qualities that are worthy of praise.

rows.pali: Tatiyanti gaṇanānupubbatā tatiyaṃ, idaṃ tatiyaṃ samāpajjatītipi tatiyaṃ.
rows.english: Third: it is the third in the numerical series; and it is third because it is entered upon third.

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana vuttaṃ "ekaṅgavippahīnaṃ duvaṅgasamannāgata"nti, ettha pītiyā pahānavasena ekaṅgavippahīnatā veditabbā.
rows.english: 178. Then it was said, which abandons one factor, possesses two factors (§153): here the abandoning of the one factor should be understood as the abandoning of happiness.

rows.pali: Sā panesā dutiyajjhānassa vitakkavicārā viya appanākkhaṇeyeva pahīyati.
rows.english: But that is abandoned only at the moment of absorption, as applied thought and sustained thought are at that of the second jhāna;

rows.pali: Tena nassa sā pahānaṅganti vuccati.
rows.english: hence it is called its factor of abandoning.

rows.pali: Sukhaṃ cittekaggatāti imesaṃ pana dvinnaṃ uppattivasena duvaṅgasamannāgatatā veditabbā.
rows.english: 179. The possession of the two factors should be understood as the arising of the two, namely, bliss and unification.

rows.pali: Tasmā yaṃ vibhaṅge "jhānanti upekkhā sati sampajaññaṃ sukhaṃ cittassekaggatā"ti (vibha. 591) vuttaṃ, taṃ saparikkhāraṃ jhānaṃ dassetuṃ pariyāyena vuttaṃ.
rows.english: So when it is said in the Vibhaṅga, “‘Jhāna’: equanimity, mindfulness, full awareness, bliss, unification of mind” (Vibh 260), this is said figuratively in order to show that jhāna with its equipment.

rows.pali: Ṭhapetvā pana upekkhāsatisampajaññāni nippariyāyena upanijjhānalakkhaṇappattānaṃ aṅgānaṃ vasena duvaṅgikamevetaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But, excepting the equanimity and mindfulness and full awareness, this jhāna has literally only two factors qua factors that have attained to the characteristic of lighting (see §119),

rows.pali: Yathāha – "katamaṃ tasmiṃ samaye duvaṅgikaṃ jhānaṃ hoti, sukhaṃ cittassekaggatā"ti (dha. sa. 163; vibha. 624).
rows.english: according as it is said, “What is the jhāna of two factors on that occasion? It is bliss and unification of mind” (Vibh 264).

rows.pali: Sesaṃ paṭhamajjhāne vuttanayameva.
rows.english: The rest is as in the case of the first jhāna.

rows.pali: 87.Evamadhigate pana tasmiṃpi vuttanayeneva pañcahākārehi ciṇṇavasinā hutvā paguṇatatiyajjhānato vuṭṭhāya "ayaṃ samāpatti āsannapītipaccatthikā, 'yadeva tattha sukhamiti cetaso ābhogo, etenetaṃ oḷārikaṃ akkhāyatī'ti (dī. ni. 1.96) evaṃ vuttassa sukhassa oḷārikattā aṅgadubbalā"ti ca tattha dosaṃ disvā catutthaṃ jhānaṃ santato manasikatvā tatiyajjhāne nikantiṃ pariyādāya catutthādhigamāya yogo kātabbo.
rows.english: 180. Once this has been obtained in this way, and once he has mastery in the five ways already described, then on emerging from the now familiar third jhāna, he can regard the flaws in it thus: “This attainment is threatened by the nearness of happiness; ‘Whatever there is in it of mental concern about bliss proclaims its grossness’ (D I 37; see Ch. IX, n. 20), and its factors are weakened by the grossness of the bliss so expressed. ” He can bring the fourth jhāna to mind as quieter and so end his attachment to the third jhāna and set about doing what is needed for attaining the fourth.

rows.pali: Athassa yadā tatiyajjhānato vuṭṭhāya satassa sampajānassa jhānaṅgāni paccavekkhato cetasikasomanassasaṅkhātaṃ sukhaṃ oḷārikato upaṭṭhāti, upekkhāvedanā ceva cittekaggatā ca santato upaṭṭhāti, tadāssa oḷārikaṅgappahānāya santaaṅgapaṭilābhāya ca tadeva nimittaṃ "pathavī pathavī"ti punappunaṃ manasikaroto "idāni catutthaṃ jhānaṃ uppajjissatī"ti bhavaṅgaṃ upacchinditvā tadeva pathavīkasiṇaṃ ārammaṇaṃ katvā manodvārāvajjanaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: 181. When he has emerged from the third jhāna, the bliss, in other words, the mental joy, appears gross to him as he reviews the jhāna factors with mindfulness and full awareness, while the equanimity as feeling and the unification of mind appear peaceful. Then, as he brings that same sign to mind as “earth, earth” again and again with the purpose of abandoning the gross factor and obtaining the peaceful factors, [knowing] “now the fourth jhāna will arise,” there arises in him mind-door adverting with that same earth kasiṇa as its object, interrupting the life-continuum.

rows.pali: Tato tasmiṃyevārammaṇe cattāri pañca vā javanāni uppajjanti, yesaṃ avasāne ekaṃ rūpāvacaraṃ catutthajjhānikaṃ, sesāni vuttappakārāneva kāmāvacarāni.
rows.english: After that either four or five impulsions impel on that same object, [165] the last one of which is an impulsion of the fine-material sphere belonging to the fourth jhāna. The rest are of the kinds already stated (§74).

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana viseso, yasmā sukhavedanā adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya āsevanapaccayena paccayo na hoti, catutthajjhāne ca adukkhamasukhāya vedanāya uppajjitabbaṃ, tasmā tāni upekkhāvedanāsampayuttāni honti.
rows.english: 182. But there is this difference: blissful (pleasant) feeling is not a condition, as repetition condition, for neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, and [the preliminary work] must be aroused in the case of the fourth jhāna with neither- painful-nor-pleasant feeling; consequently these [consciousnesses of the preliminary work] are associated with neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling,

rows.pali: Upekkhāsampayuttattāyeva cettha pītipi parihāyatīti.
rows.english: and here happiness vanishes simply owing to their association with equanimity.

rows.pali: Ettāvatā cesa sukhassa ca pahānā dukkhassa ca pahānā pubbeva somanassadomanassānaṃ atthaṅgamā adukkhamasukhaṃ upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṃ catutthaṃ jhānaṃ upasampajja viharati (dī. ni. 1.232; dha. sa. 165).
rows.english: [THE FOURTH JHĀNA] 183. And at this point, “With the abandoning of pleasure and pain and with the previous disappearance of joy and grief he enters upon and dwells in the fourth jhāna, which has neither-pain-nor-pleasure and has purity of mindfulness due to equanimity” (Vibh 245),

rows.pali: Evamanena ekaṅgavippahīnaṃ duvaṅgasamannāgataṃ tividhakalyāṇaṃ dasalakkhaṇasampannaṃ catutthaṃ jhānaṃ adhigataṃ hoti pathavīkasiṇaṃ.
rows.english: and so he has attained the fourth jhāna, which abandons one factor, possesses two factors, is good in three ways, possesses ten characteristics, and is of the earth kasiṇa.

rows.pali: 88.Tattha sukhassa ca pahānā dukkhassa ca pahānāti kāyikasukhassa ca kāyikadukkhassa ca pahānā.
rows.english: 184. Herein, with the abandoning of pleasure and pain: with the abandoning of bodily pleasure and bodily pain.

rows.pali: Pubbevāti tañca kho pubbeva, na catutthajjhānakkhaṇe.
rows.english: With the previous: which took place before, not in the moment of the fourth jhāna.

rows.pali: Somanassadomanassānaṃ atthaṅgamāti cetasikasukhassa ca cetasikadukkhassa cāti imesampi dvinnaṃ pubbeva atthaṅgamā, pahānā icceva vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Disappearance of joy and grief: with the previous disappearance of the two, that is, mental bliss (pleasure) and mental pain; with the abandoning, is what is meant.

rows.pali: Kadā pana nesaṃ pahānaṃ hotīti.
rows.english: 185. But when does the abandoning of these take place?

rows.pali: Catunnaṃ jhānānaṃ upacārakkhaṇe.
rows.english: At the moment of access of the four jhānas.

rows.pali: Somanassañhi catutthajjhānassa upacārakkhaṇeyeva pahīyati.
rows.english: For [mental] joy is only abandoned at the moment of the fourth-jhāna access,

rows.pali: Dukkhadomanassasukhāni paṭhamadutiyatatiyajjhānānaṃ upacārakkhaṇesu.
rows.english: while [bodily] pain, [mental] grief, and [bodily] bliss (pleasure) are abandoned respectively at the moments of access of the first, second, and third jhānas.

rows.pali: Evametesaṃ pahānakkamena avuttānampi indriyavibhaṅge pana indriyānaṃ uddesakkameneva idhāpi vuttānaṃ sukhadukkhasomanassadomanassānaṃ pahānaṃ veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: So although the order in which they are abandoned is not actually mentioned, nevertheless the abandoning of the pleasure, pain, joy, and grief, is stated here according to the order in which the faculties are summarized in the Indriya Vibhaṅga (Vibh 122).

rows.pali: Yadi panetāni tassa tassa jhānassa upacārakkhaṇeyeva pahīyanti, atha kasmā "kattha cuppannaṃ dukkhindriyaṃ aparisesaṃ nirujjhati, idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vivicceva kāmehipi - pe - paṭhamaṃ jhānaṃ upasampajja viharati.
rows.english: 186. But if these are only abandoned at the moments of access of the several jhānas, why is their cessation said to. take place in the jhāna itself in the following passage: “And where does the arisen pain faculty cease without remainder? Here, bhikkhus, quite secluded from sense desires, secluded from unprofitable things, a bhikkhu enters upon and dwells in the first jhāna, which is … born of seclusion.

rows.pali: Ettha cuppannaṃ dukkhindriyaṃ aparisesaṃ nirujjhati.
rows.english: It is here that the arisen pain faculty ceases without remainder …

rows.pali: Kattha cuppannaṃ domanassindriyaṃ sukhindriyaṃ somanassindriyaṃ aparisesaṃ nirujjhati, idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sukhassa ca pahānā - pe - catutthaṃ jhānaṃ upasampajja viharati, ettha cuppannaṃ somanassindriyaṃ aparisesaṃ nirujjhatī"ti (saṃ. ni. 5.510) evaṃ jhānesveva nirodho vuttoti?
rows.english: Where does the arisen grief faculty [cease without remainder? … in the second jhāna] … Where does the arisen pleasure faculty [cease without remainder? … in the third jhāna] … Where does the arisen joy faculty cease without remainder? Here, bhikkhus, with the abandoning of pleasure and pain [and with the previous disappearance of joy and grief] a bhikkhu enters upon and dwells in the fourth jhāna, which … has mindfulness purified by equanimity. It is here that the arisen joy faculty ceases without remainder” (S V 213–15). It is said in that way there referring to reinforced cessation.

rows.pali: Atisayanirodhattā.
Atisayanirodho hi nesaṃ paṭhamajjhānādīsu, na nirodhoyeva.
rows.english: For in the first jhāna, etc., it is their reinforced cessation, not just their cessation, that takes place.

rows.pali: Nirodhoyeva pana upacārakkhaṇe, nātisayanirodho.
rows.english: At the moment of access it is just their cessation, not their reinforced cessation, that takes place.

rows.pali: Tathā hi nānāvajjane paṭhamajjhānupacāre niruddhassāpi dukkhindriyassa ḍaṃsamakasādisamphassena vā visamāsanupatāpena vā siyā uppatti, na tveva antoappanāyaṃ.
rows.english: 187. For accordingly, during the first jhāna access, which has multiple adverting, there could be rearising of the [bodily] pain faculty49 due to contact with gadflies, flies, etc. or the discomfort of an uneven seat, though that pain faculty had already ceased, but not so during absorption.

rows.pali: Upacāre vā niruddhampetaṃ na suṭṭhu niruddhaṃ hoti, paṭipakkhena avihatattā.
rows.english: Or else, though it has ceased during access, it has not absolutely ceased there since it is not quite beaten out by opposition.

rows.pali: Antoappanāyaṃ pana pītipharaṇena sabbo kāyo sukhokkanto hoti, sukhokkantakāyassa ca suṭṭhu niruddhaṃ hoti dukkhindriyaṃ, paṭipakkhena vihatattā.
rows.english: But during absorption the whole body is showered with bliss owing to pervasion by happiness. And the pain faculty has absolutely ceased in one whose body is showered with bliss, since it is beaten out then by opposition.

rows.pali: Nānāvajjaneyeva ca dutiyajjhānupacāre pahīnassa domanassindriyassa yasmā etaṃ vitakkavicārapaccayepi kāyakilamathe cittupaghāte ca sati uppajjati.
rows.english: 188.And during the second-jhāna access too, which has multiple advertings, there could be rearising of the [mental] grief faculty, although it had already ceased there, because it arises when there is bodily weariness and mental vexation, which have applied thought and sustained thought as their condition,

rows.pali: Vitakkavicārābhāve ca neva uppajjati.
rows.english: but it does not arise when applied and sustained thought are absent.

rows.pali: Yattha pana uppajjati, tattha vitakkavicārabhāve, appahīnā eva ca dutiyajjhānupacāre vitakkavicārāti tatthassa siyā uppatti, na tveva dutiyajjhāne, pahīnapaccayattā.
rows.english: When it arises, it does so in the presence of applied and sustained thought, and they are not abandoned in the second-jhāna access; but this is not so in the second jhāna itself because its conditions are abandoned there.

rows.pali: Tathā tatiyajjhānupacāre pahīnassāpi sukhindriyassa pītisamuṭṭhānapaṇītarūpaphuṭakāyassa siyā uppatti, na tveva tatiyajjhāne.
rows.english: 189. Likewise in the third-jhāna access there could be rearising of the abandoned [bodily] pleasure faculty in one whose body was pervaded by the superior materiality originated by the [consciousness associated with the] happiness. But not so in the third jhāna itself.

rows.pali: Tatiyajjhāne hi sukhassa paccayabhūtā pīti sabbaso niruddhāti.
rows.english: For in the third jhāna the happiness that is a condition for the [bodily] bliss (pleasure) has ceased entirely.

rows.pali: Tathā catutthajjhānupacāre pahīnassāpi somanassindriyassa āsannattā appanāppattāya upekkhāya abhāvena sammā anatikkantattā ca siyā uppatti, na tveva catutthajjhāne.
rows.english: Likewise in the fourth-jhāna access there could be re-arising of the abandoned [mental] joy faculty because of its nearness and because it has not been properly surmounted owing to the absence of equanimity brought to absorption strength. But not so in the fourth jhāna itself.

rows.pali: Tasmā eva ca etthuppannaṃ dukkhindriyaṃ aparisesaṃ nirujjhatīti tattha tattha aparisesaggahaṇaṃ katanti.
rows.english: And that is why in each case (§186) the words “without remainder” are included thus: “It is here that the arisen pain faculty ceases without remainder. ”

rows.pali: Etthāha "athevaṃ tassa tassa jhānassupacāre pahīnāpi etā vedanā idha kasmā samāhaṭā"ti?
rows.english: 190. Here it may be asked: Then if these kinds of feeling are abandoned in the access in this way, why are they brought in here?

rows.pali: Sukhaggahaṇatthaṃ.
rows.english: It is done so that they can be readily grasped.

rows.pali: Yā hi ayaṃ adukkhamasukhanti ettha adukkhamasukhā vedanā vuttā, sā sukhumā duviññeyyā na sakkā sukhena gahetuṃ, tasmā yathā nāma duṭṭhassa yathā vā tathā vā upasaṅkamitvā gahetuṃ asakkuṇeyyassa goṇassa sukhaggahaṇatthaṃ gopo ekasmiṃ vaje sabbā gāvo samāharati, athekekaṃ nīharanto paṭipāṭiyā āgataṃ "ayaṃ so gaṇhatha na"nti tampi gāhayati, evameva bhagavā sukhaggahaṇatthaṃ sabbā etā samāhari.
rows.english: For the neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling described here by the words “which has neither-pain-nor-pleasure” is subtle, hard to recognize and not readily grasped. So just as, when a cattle-herd50 wants to catch a refractory ox that cannot be caught at all by approaching it, he collects all the cattle into one pen [167] and lets them out one by one, and then [he says] “That is it; catch it,” and so it gets caught as well, so too the Blessed One has collected all these [five kinds of feeling] together so that they can be grasped readily;

rows.pali: Evañhi samāhaṭā etā dassetvā yaṃ neva sukhaṃ na dukkhaṃ na somanassaṃ na domanassaṃ, ayaṃ adukkhamasukhā vedanāti sakkā hoti esā gāhayituṃ.
rows.english: for when they are shown collected together in this way, then what is not [bodily] pleasure (bliss) or [bodily] pain or [mental] joy or [mental] grief can still be grasped in this way: “This is neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. ”

rows.pali: Apica adukkhamasukhāya cetovimuttiyā paccayadassanatthañcāpi etā vuttāti veditabbā.
rows.english: 191. Besides, this may be understood as said in order to show the condition for the neither-painful-nor-pleasant mind-deliverance.

rows.pali: Dukkhappahānādayo hi tassā paccayā.
rows.english: For the abandoning of [bodily] pain, etc., are conditions for that,

rows.pali: Yathāha – "cattāro kho, āvuso, paccayā adukkhamasukhāya cetovimuttiyā samāpattiyā.
rows.english: according as it is said: “There are four conditions, friend, for the attainment of the neither-painful-nor-pleasant mind- deliverance.

rows.pali: Idhāvuso, bhikkhu sukhassa ca pahānā - pe - catutthaṃ jhānaṃ upasampajja viharati.
rows.english: Here, friend, with the abandoning of pleasure and pain and with the previous disappearance of joy and grief a bhikkhu enters upon and dwells in the fourth jhāna … equanimity.

rows.pali: Ime khvāvuso, cattāro paccayā adukkhamasukhāya cetovimuttiyā samāpattiyā"ti (ma. ni. 1.458).
rows.english: These are the four conditions for the attainment of the neither-painful-nor-pleasant mind-deliverance” (M I 296).

rows.pali: Yathā vā aññattha pahīnāpi sakkāyadiṭṭhiādayo tatiyamaggassa vaṇṇabhaṇanatthaṃ tattha pahīnāti vuttā, evaṃ vaṇṇabhaṇanatthampetassa jhānassetā idha vuttātipi veditabbā.
rows.english: 192. Or alternatively, just as, although mistaken view of individuality, etc., have already been abandoned in the earlier paths, they are nevertheless mentioned as abandoned in the description of the third path for the purpose of recommending it (cf. §155), so too these kinds of feeling can be understood as mentioned here for the purpose of recommending this jhāna.

rows.pali: Paccayaghātena vā ettha rāgadosānamatidūrabhāvaṃ dassetumpetā vuttāti veditabbā.
rows.english: Or alternatively, they can be understood as mentioned for the purpose of showing that greed and hate are very far away owing to the removal of their conditions;

rows.pali: Etāsu hi sukhaṃ somanassassa paccayo, somanassaṃ rāgassa.
rows.english: for of these, pleasure (bliss) is a condition for joy, and joy for greed;

rows.pali: Dukkhaṃ domanassassa paccayo, domanassaṃ dosassa.
rows.english: pain is a condition for grief and grief for hate.

rows.pali: Sukhādighātena cassa sappaccayā rāgadosā hatāti atidūre hontīti.
rows.english: So with the removal of pleasure (bliss), etc., greed and hate are very far away since they are removed along with their conditions.

rows.pali: Adukkhamasukhanti dukkhābhāvena adukkhaṃ.
rows.english: 193. Which has neither-pain-nor-pleasure: no pain owing to absence of pain;

rows.pali: Sukhābhāvena asukhaṃ.
rows.english: no pleasure owing to absence of pleasure (bliss).

rows.pali: Etenettha dukkhasukhapaṭipakkhabhūtaṃ tatiyavedanaṃ dīpeti, na dukkhasukhābhāvamattaṃ.
rows.english: By this he indicates the third kind of feeling that is in opposition both to pain and to pleasure, not the mere absence of pain and pleasure.

rows.pali: Tatiyavedanā nāma adukkhamasukhā, upekkhātipi vuccati.
rows.english: This third kind of feeling named neither-pain-nor-pleasure is also called “equanimity.”

rows.pali: Sā iṭṭhāniṭṭhaviparītānubhavanalakkhaṇā, majjhattarasā, avibhūtapaccupaṭṭhānā, sukhadukkhanirodhapadaṭṭhānāti veditabbā.
rows.english: It has the characteristic of experiencing what is contrary to both the desirable and the undesirable. Its function is neutral. Its manifestation is unevident. Its proximate cause should be understood as the cessation of pleasure (bliss).

rows.pali: 89.Upekkhāsatipārisuddhinti upekkhāya janitasatiyā pārisuddhiṃ.
rows.english: 194. And has purity of mindfulness due to equanimity: has purity of mindfulness brought about by equanimity.

rows.pali: Imasmiñhi jhāne suparisuddhā sati, yā ca tassā satiyā pārisuddhi, sā upekkhāya katā, na aññena.
rows.english: For the mindfulness in this jhāna is quite purified, and its purification is effected by equanimity, not by anything else.

rows.pali: Tasmā etaṃ "upekkhāsatipārisuddhi"nti vuccati.
rows.english: That is why it is said to have purity of mindfulness due to equanimity.

rows.pali: Vibhaṅgepi vuttaṃ "ayaṃ sati imāya upekkhāya visadā hoti parisuddhā pariyodātā.
rows.english: Also it is said in the Vibhaṅga: “This mindfulness is cleared, purified, clarified, by equanimity;

rows.pali: Tena vuccati upekkhāsatipārisuddhī"ti (vibha. 597).
rows.english: hence it is said to have purity of mindfulness due to equanimity” (Vibh 261).

rows.pali: Yāya ca upekkhāya ettha satiyā pārisuddhi hoti, sā atthato tatramajjhattatātiveditabbā.
rows.english: And the equanimity due to which there comes to be this purity of mindfulness should be understood as specific neutrality in meaning.

rows.pali: Na kevalañcettha tāya satiyeva parisuddhā, apica kho sabbepi sampayuttadhammā, satisīsena pana desanā vuttā.
rows.english: And not only mindfulness is purified by it here, but also all associated states. However, the teaching is given under the heading of mindfulness.

rows.pali: Tattha kiñcāpi ayaṃ upekkhā heṭṭhāpi tīsu jhānesu vijjati.
rows.english: 195.Herein, this equanimity exists in the three lower jhānas too;

rows.pali: Yathā pana divā sūriyappabhābhibhavā sommabhāvena ca attano upakārakattena vā sabhāgāya rattiyā alābhā divā vijjamānāpi candalekhā aparisuddhā hoti apariyodātā, evamayampi tatramajjhattupekkhācandalekhā vitakkādipaccanīkadhammatejābhibhavā sabhāgāya ca upekkhāvedanārattiyā appaṭilābhā vijjamānāpi paṭhamādijjhānabhedesu aparisuddhā hoti.
rows.english: but just as, although a crescent moon exists by day but is not purified or clear since it is outshone by the sun’s radiance in the daytime or since it is deprived of the night, which is its ally owing to gentleness and owing to helpfulness to it, so too, this crescent moon of equanimity consisting in specific neutrality exists in the first jhāna, etc., but it is not purified since it is outshone by the glare of the opposing states consisting in applied thought, etc., and since it is deprived of the night of equanimity-as-feeling for its ally;

rows.pali: Tassā ca aparisuddhāya divā aparisuddhacandalekhāya pabhā viya sahajātāpi satiādayo aparisuddhāva honti.
rows.english: and because it is not purified, the conascent mindfulness and other states are not purified either, like the unpurified crescent moon’s radiance by day.

rows.pali: Tasmā tesu ekampi "upekkhāsatipārisuddhi"nti na vuttaṃ.
rows.english: That is why no one among these [first three jhānas] is said to have purity of mindfulness due to equanimity.

rows.pali: Idha pana vitakkādipaccanīkadhammatejābhibhavābhāvā sabhāgāya ca upekkhāvedanārattiyā paṭilābhā ayaṃ tatramajjhattupekkhācandalekhā ativiya parisuddhā.
rows.english: But here this crescent moon consisting in specific neutrality is utterly pure because it is not outshone by the glare of the opposing states consisting in applied thought, etc., and because it has the night of equanimity-as-feeling for its ally.

rows.pali: Tassā parisuddhattā parisuddhacandalekhāya pabhā viya sahajātāpi satiādayo parisuddhā honti pariyodātā.
rows.english: And since it is purified, the conascent mindfulness and other states are purified and clear also, like the purified crescent moon’s radiance.

rows.pali: Tasmā idameva "upekkhāsatipārisuddhi"nti vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: That, it should be understood, is why only this jhāna is said to have purity of mindfulness due to equanimity.

rows.pali: Catutthanti gaṇanānupubbatā catutthaṃ.
rows.english: 196.Fourth: it is fourth in numerical series;

rows.pali: Idaṃ catutthaṃ samāpajjatītipi catutthaṃ.
rows.english: and it is fourth because it is entered upon fourth.

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana vuttaṃ "ekaṅgavippahīnaṃ duvaṅgasamannāgata"nti, tattha somanassassa pahānavasena ekaṅgavippahīnatā veditabbā.
rows.english: 197. Then it was said, which abandons one factor, possesses two factors (§183); here the abandoning of the one factor should be understood as the abandoning of joy.

rows.pali: Tañca pana somanassaṃ ekavīthiyaṃ purimajavanesuyeva pahīyati.
rows.english: But that joy is actually abandoned in the first impulsions of the same cognitive series (cf. §185).

rows.pali: Tenassa taṃ pahānaṅganti vuccati.
rows.english: Hence it is called its factor of abandoning.

rows.pali: Upekkhāvedanā cittassekaggatāti imesaṃ pana dvinnaṃ uppattivasena duvaṅgasamannāgatatā veditabbā.
rows.english: The possession of the two factors should be understood as the arising of the two, namely, equanimity as feeling and unification of mind.

rows.pali: Sesaṃ paṭhamajjhāne vuttanayameva.
rows.english: The rest is as stated in the case of the first jhāna.

rows.pali: Esa tāva catukkajjhāne nayo.
rows.english: This, in the first place, is according to the fourfold reckoning of jhāna.

rows.pali: 90.Pañcakajjhānaṃ pana nibbattentena paguṇapaṭhamajjhānato vuṭṭhāya "ayaṃ samāpatti āsannanīvaraṇapaccatthikā, vitakkassa oḷārikattā aṅgadubbalā"ti ca tattha dosaṃ disvā dutiyajjhānaṃ santato manasikaritvā paṭhamajjhāne nikantiṃ pariyādāya dutiyādhigamāya yogo kātabbo.
rows.english: 198. When, however, he is developing fivefold jhāna, then, on emerging from the now familiar first jhāna, he can regard the flaws in it in this way: “This attainment is threatened by the nearness of the hindrances, and its factors are weakened by the grossness of applied thought. ” [169] He can bring the second jhāna to mind as quieter and so end his attachment to the first jhāna and set about doing what is needed for attaining the second.

rows.pali: Athassa yadā paṭhamajjhānā vuṭṭhāya satassa sampajānassa jhānaṅgāni paccavekkhato vitakkamattaṃ oḷārikato upaṭṭhāti, vicārādayo santato.
rows.english: 199. Now, he emerges from the first jhāna mindfully and fully aware; and only applied thought appears gross to him as he reviews the jhāna factors, while the sustained thought, etc., appear peaceful.

rows.pali: Tadāssa oḷārikaṅgappahānāya santaṅgapaṭilābhāya ca tadeva nimittaṃ "pathavī pathavī"ti punappunaṃ manasikaroto vuttanayeneva dutiyajjhānaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: Then, as he brings that same sign to mind as “earth, earth” again and again with the purpose of abandoning the gross factor and obtaining the peaceful factors, the second jhāna arises in him in the way already described.

rows.pali: Tassa vitakkamattameva pahānaṅgaṃ.
rows.english: Its factor of abandoning is applied thought only.

rows.pali: Vicārādīni cattāri samannāgataṅgāni.
rows.english: The four beginning with sustained thought are the factors that it possesses.

rows.pali: Sesaṃ vuttappakārameva.
rows.english: The rest is as already stated.

rows.pali: Evamadhigate pana tasmimpi vuttanayeneva pañcahākārehi ciṇṇavasinā hutvā paguṇadutiyajjhānato vuṭṭhāya "ayaṃ samāpatti āsannavitakkapaccatthikā, vicārassa oḷārikattā aṅgadubbalā"ti ca tattha dosaṃ disvā tatiyaṃ jhānaṃ santato manasikaritvā dutiyajjhāne nikantiṃ pariyādāya tatiyādhigamāya yogo kātabbo.
rows.english: 200. When this has been obtained in this way, and once he has mastery in the five ways already described, then on emerging from the now familiar second jhāna he can regard the flaws in it in this way: “This attainment is threatened by the nearness of applied thought, and its factors are weakened by the grossness of sustained thought. ” He can bring the third jhāna to mind as quieter and so end his attachment to the second jhāna and set about doing what is needed for attaining the third.

rows.pali: Athassa yadā dutiyajjhānato vuṭṭhāya satassa sampajānassa jhānaṅgāni paccavekkhato vicāramattaṃ oḷārikato upaṭṭhāti, pītiādīni santato.
rows.english: 201. Now, he emerges from the second jhāna mindfully and fully aware; only sustained thought appears gross to him as he reviews the jhāna factors, while happiness, etc., appear peaceful.

rows.pali: Tadāssa oḷārikaṅgappahānāya santaṅgapaṭilābhāya ca tadeva nimittaṃ "pathavī pathavī"ti punappunaṃ manasikaroto vuttanayeneva tatiyaṃ jhānaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: Then, as he brings that same sign to mind as “earth, earth” again and again with the purpose of abandoning the gross factor and obtaining the peaceful factors, the third jhāna arises in him in the way already described.

rows.pali: Tassa vicāramattameva pahānaṅgaṃ catukkanayassa dutiyajjhāne viya pītiādīni tīṇi samannāgataṅgāni.
rows.english: Its factor of abandoning is sustained thought only. The three beginning with happiness, as in the second jhāna in the fourfold reckoning, are the factors that it possesses.

rows.pali: Sesaṃ vuttappakārameva.
rows.english: The rest is as already stated.

rows.pali: Iti yaṃ catukkanaye dutiyaṃ, taṃ dvidhā bhinditvā pañcakanaye dutiyañceva tatiyañca hoti.
rows.english: 202. So that which is the second in the fourfold reckoning becomes the second and third in the fivefold reckoning by being divided into two.

rows.pali: Yāni ca tattha tatiyacatutthāni, tāni ca catutthapañcamāni honti.
rows.english: And those which are the third and fourth in the former reckoning become the fourth and fifth in this reckoning.

rows.pali: Paṭhamaṃ paṭhamamevāti.
rows.english: The first remains the first in each case.

rows.pali: 52.Tattha ananurūpo nāma aṭṭhārasannaṃ dosānaṃ aññatarena samannāgato.
rows.english: 2.Herein, one that is unfavourable has anyone of eighteen faults.

rows.pali: Tatrime aṭṭhārasa dosā – mahattaṃ, navattaṃ, jiṇṇattaṃ, panthanissitattaṃ, soṇḍī, paṇṇaṃ, pupphaṃ, phalaṃ, patthanīyatā, nagarasannissitatā, dārusannissitatā, khettasannissitatā, visabhāgānaṃ puggalānaṃ atthitā, paṭṭanasannissitatā, paccantasannissitatā, rajjasīmasannissitatā, asappāyatā, kalyāṇamittānaṃ alābhoti imesaṃ aṭṭhārasannaṃ dosānaṃ aññatarena dosena samannāgato ananurūpo nāma.
rows.english: These are: (1) largeness, (2) newness, (3) dilapidatedness, (4) a nearby road, (5) a pond, (6) [edible] leaves, (7) flowers, (8) fruits, (9) famousness, (10) a nearby city, (11) nearby timber trees, (12) nearby arable fields, (13) presence of incompatible persons, (14) a nearby port of entry, (15) nearness to the border countries, (16) nearness to the frontier of a kingdom, (17) unsuitability, (18) lack of good friends. [119] One with any of these faults is not favourable.

rows.pali: Na tattha vihātabbaṃ.
rows.english: He should not live there.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Mahāvihāre tāva bahū nānāchandā sannipatanti, te aññamaññaṃ paṭiviruddhatāya vattaṃ na karonti.
rows.english: 3.1.Firstly, people with varying aims collect in a large monastery. They conflict with each other and so neglect the duties.

rows.pali: Bodhiyaṅgaṇādīni asammaṭṭhāneva honti.
rows.english: The Enlightenment-tree terrace, etc., remain unswept,

rows.pali: Anupaṭṭhāpitaṃ pānīyaṃ paribhojanīyaṃ.
rows.english: the water for drinking and washing is not set out.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ gocaragāme piṇḍāya carissāmīti pattacīvaramādāya nikkhanto sace passati vattaṃ vā akataṃ pānīyaghaṭaṃ vā rittaṃ, athānena vattaṃ kātabbaṃ hoti, pānīyaṃ upaṭṭhāpetabbaṃ.
rows.english: So if he thinks, “I shall go to the alms-resort village for alms” and takes his bowl and robe and sets out, perhaps he sees that the duties have not been done or that a drinking-water pot is empty, and so the duty has to be done by him unexpectedly. Drinking water must be maintained.

rows.pali: Akaronto vattabhede dukkaṭaṃ āpajjati.
rows.english: By not doing it he would commit a wrongdoing in the breach of a duty.

rows.pali: Karontassa kālo atikkamati, atidivā paviṭṭho niṭṭhitāya bhikkhāya kiñci na labhati.
rows.english: But if he does it, he loses time. He arrives too late at the village and gets nothing because the alms giving is finished.

rows.pali: Paṭisallānagatopi sāmaṇeradaharabhikkhūnaṃ uccāsaddena saṅghakammehi ca vikkhipati.
rows.english: Also, when he goes into retreat, he is distracted by the loud noises of novices and young bhikkhus, and by acts of the Community [being carried out].

rows.pali: Yattha pana sabbaṃ vattaṃ katameva hoti, avasesāpi ca saṅghaṭṭanā natthi. Evarūpe mahāvihārepi vihātabbaṃ.
rows.english: However, he can live in a large monastery where all the duties are done and where there are none of the other disturbances.

rows.pali: Navavihāre bahu navakammaṃ hoti, akarontaṃ ujjhāyanti.
rows.english: 4. 2. In a new monastery there is much new building activity. People criticize someone who takes no part in it.

rows.pali: Yattha pana bhikkhū evaṃ vadanti "āyasmā yathāsukhaṃ samaṇadhammaṃ karotu, mayaṃ navakammaṃ karissāmā"ti evarūpe vihātabbaṃ.
rows.english: But he can live in such a monastery where the bhikkhus say, “Let the venerable one do the ascetic’s duties as much as he likes. We shall see to the building work. ”

rows.pali: Jiṇṇavihāre pana bahu paṭijaggitabbaṃ hoti, antamaso attano senāsanamattampi appaṭijaggantaṃ ujjhāyanti, paṭijaggantassa kammaṭṭhānaṃ parihāyati.
rows.english: 5.3. In a dilapidated monastery there is much that needs repair. People criticize someone who does not see about the repairing of at least his own lodging. When he sees to the repairs, his meditation subject suffers.

rows.pali: Panthanissite mahāpathavihāre rattindivaṃ āgantukā sannipatanti.
rows.english: 6.4. In a monastery with a nearby road, by a main street, visitors keep arriving night and day.

rows.pali: Vikāle āgatānaṃ attano senāsanaṃ datvā rukkhamūle vā pāsāṇapiṭṭhe vā vasitabbaṃ hoti.
rows.english: He has to give up his own lodging to those who come late, and he has to go and live at the root of a tree or on top of a rock.

rows.pali: Punadivasepi evamevāti kammaṭṭhānassa okāso na hoti.
rows.english: And next day it is the same. So there is no opportunity [to practice] his meditation subject.

rows.pali: Yattha pana evarūpo āgantukasambādho na hoti, tattha vihātabbaṃ.
rows.english: But he can live in one where there is no such disturbance by visitors.

rows.pali: Soṇḍī nāma pāsāṇapokkharaṇī hoti, tattha pānīyatthaṃ mahājano samosarati, nagaravāsīnaṃ rājakulūpakattherānaṃ antevāsikā rajanakammatthāya āgacchanti, tesaṃ bhājanadārudoṇikādīni pucchantānaṃ asuke ca asuke ca ṭhāneti dassetabbāni honti, evaṃ sabbakālampi niccabyāvaṭo hoti.
rows.english: 7.5. A pond is a rock pool. Numbers of people come there for drinking water. Pupils of city-dwelling elders supported by the royal family come to do dyeing work. When they ask for vessels, wood, tubs, etc., they must be shown where these things are. So he is kept all the time on the alert.

rows.pali: Yattha nānāvidhaṃ sākapaṇṇaṃ hoti, tatthassa kammaṭṭhānaṃ gahetvā divāvihāraṃ nisinnassāpi santike sākahārikā gāyamānā paṇṇaṃ uccinantiyo visabhāgasaddasaṅghaṭṭanena kammaṭṭhānantarāyaṃ karonti.
rows.english: 8.6. If he goes with his meditation subject to sit by day where there are many sorts of edible leaves, then women vegetable-gatherers, singing as they pick leaves nearby, endanger his meditation subject by disturbing it with sounds of the opposite sex.

rows.pali: Yattha pana nānāvidhā mālāgacchā supupphitā honti, tatrāpi tādisoyeva upaddavo.
rows.english: 7. And where there are many sorts of flowering shrubs in bloom there is the same danger too.

rows.pali: Yattha nānāvidhaṃ ambajambupanasādiphalaṃ hoti, tattha phalatthikā āgantvā yācanti, adentassa kujjhanti, balakkārena vā gaṇhanti, sāyanhasamaye vihāramajjhe caṅkamantena te disvā "kiṃ upāsakā evaṃ karothā"ti vuttā yathāruci akkosanti.
rows.english: 9.8. Where there are many sorts of fruits such as mangoes, rose-apples and jak-fruits, people who want fruits come and ask for them, and they get angry if he does not give them any, or they take them by force. When walking in the monastery in the evening he sees them and asks, “Why do you do so, lay followers? ” they abuse him as they please

rows.pali: Avāsāyapissa parakkamanti.
rows.english: and even try to evict him.

rows.pali: Patthanīye pana leṇasammate dakkhiṇagirihatthikucchicetiyagiricittalapabbatasadise vihāre viharantaṃ ayamarahāti sambhāvetvā vanditukāmā manussā samantā osaranti, tenassa na phāsu hoti, yassa pana taṃ sappāyaṃ hoti, tena divā aññatra gantvā rattiṃ vasitabbaṃ.
rows.english: 10. 9. When he lives in a monastery that is famous and renowned in the world, like Dakkhiṇagiri1 Hatthikucchi, Cetiyagiri or Cittalapabbata, there are always people coming who want to pay homage to him, supposing that he is an Arahant, which inconveniences him. But if it suits him, he can live there at night and go elsewhere by day.

rows.pali: Nagarasannissite visabhāgārammaṇāni āpāthamāgacchanti, kumbhadāsiyopi ghaṭehi nighaṃsantiyo gacchanti, okkamitvā maggaṃ na denti, issaramanussāpi vihāramajjhe sāṇiṃ parikkhipitvā nisīdanti.
rows.english: 11.10. In one with a nearby city objects of the opposite sex come into focus. Women-pot carriers go by bumping into him with their jars and giving no room to pass. Also important people spread out carpets in the middle of the monastery and sit down.

rows.pali: Dārusannissaye pana yattha kaṭṭhāni ca dabbupakaraṇarukkhā ca santi, tattha kaṭṭhahārikā pubbe vuttasākapupphahārikā viya aphāsuṃ karonti, vihāre rukkhā santi, te chinditvā gharāni karissāmāti manussā āgantvā chindanti.
rows.english: 12. 11. One with nearby timber trees where there are timber trees and osiers useful for making framework is inconvenient because of the wood-gatherers there, like the gatherers of branches and fruits already mentioned.

rows.pali: Sace sāyanhasamayaṃ padhānagharā nikkhamitvā vihāramajjhe caṅkamanto te disvā "kiṃ upāsakā evaṃ karothā"ti vadati, yathāruci akkosanti, avāsāyapissa parakkamanti.
rows.english: If there are trees in a monastery, people come and cut them down to build houses with. When he has come out of his meditation room in the evening and is walking up and down in the monastery, if he sees them and asks, “Why do you do so, lay followers? ” they abuse him as they please and even try to evict him.

rows.pali: Yo pana khettasannissito hoti samantā khettehi parivārito, tattha manussā vihāramajjheyeva khalaṃ katvā dhaññaṃ maddanti, pamukhesu sayanti, aññampi bahuṃ aphāsuṃ karonti.
rows.english: 13.12. People make use of one with nearby arable fields, quite surrounded by fields. They make a threshing floor in the middle of the monastery itself. They thresh corn there, dry it in the forecourts,2 and cause great inconvenience.

rows.pali: Yatrāpi mahāsaṅghabhogo hoti, ārāmikā kulānaṃ gāvo rundhanti, udakavāraṃ paṭisedhenti, manussā vīhisīsaṃ gahetvā "passatha tumhākaṃ ārāmikānaṃ kamma"nti saṅghassa dassenti.
rows.english: And where there is extensive property belonging to the Community, the monastery attendants impound cattle belonging to families and deny the water supply [to their crops]. Then people bring an ear of paddy and show it to the Community saying “Look at your monastery attendants’ work.”

rows.pali: Tena tena kāraṇena rājarājamahāmattānaṃ gharadvāraṃ gantabbaṃ hoti, ayampi khettasannissiteneva saṅgahito.
rows.english: For one reason or another he has to go to the portals of the king or the king’s ministers. This [matter of property belonging to the Community] is included by [a monastery that is] near arable fields.

rows.pali: Visabhāgānaṃ puggalānaṃ atthitāti yattha aññamaññaṃ visabhāgaverī bhikkhū viharanti, ye kalahaṃ karontā mā, bhante, evaṃ karothāti vāriyamānā etassa paṃsukūlikassa āgatakālato paṭṭhāya naṭṭhāmhāti vattāro bhavanti.
rows.english: 14. 13. Presence of incompatible persons: where there are bhikkhus living who are incompatible and mutually hostile, when they clash and it is protested, “Venerable sirs, do not do so,” they exclaim, “We no longer count now that this refuse-rag wearer has come. ”

rows.pali: Yopi udakapaṭṭanaṃ vā thalapaṭṭanaṃ vā nissito hoti, tattha abhiṇhaṃ nāvāhi ca satthehi ca āgatamanussā okāsaṃ detha, pānīyaṃ detha, loṇaṃ dethāti ghaṭṭayantā aphāsuṃ karonti.
rows.english: 15. 14. One with a nearby water port of entry or land port of entry3 is made inconvenient by people constantly arriving respectively by ship or by caravan and crowding round, asking for space or for drinking water or salt.

rows.pali: Paccantasannissite pana manussā buddhādīsu appasannā honti.
rows.english: 16.15. In the case of one near the border countries, people have no trust in the Buddha, etc., there.

rows.pali: Rajjasīmasannissite rājabhayaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 16. In one near the frontier of a kingdom there is fear of kings.

rows.pali: Tañhi padesaṃ eko rājā na mayhaṃ vase vattatīti paharati, itaropi na mayhaṃ vase vattatīti.
rows.english: For perhaps one king attacks that place, thinking, “It does not submit to my rule,” and the other does likewise, thinking, “It does not submit to my rule.”

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ bhikkhu kadāci imassa rañño vijite vicarati, kadāci etassa.
rows.english: A bhikkhu lives there when it is conquered by one king and when it is conquered by the other.

rows.pali: Atha naṃ "carapuriso aya"nti maññamānā anayabyasanaṃ pāpenti.
rows.english: Then they suspect him of spying, and they bring about his undoing.

rows.pali: Asappāyatāti visabhāgarūpādiārammaṇasamosaraṇena vā amanussapariggahitatāya vā asappāyatā.
rows.english: 17.17. Unsuitability is that due to the risk of encountering visible data, etc., of the opposite sex as objects or to haunting by non-human beings.

rows.pali: Tatridaṃ vatthu.
rows.english: Here is a story.

rows.pali: Eko kira thero araññe vasati.
rows.english: An elder lived in a forest, it seems.

rows.pali: Athassa ekā yakkhinī paṇṇasāladvāre ṭhatvā gāyi.
rows.english: Then an ogress stood in the door of his leaf hut and sang.

rows.pali: So nikkhamitvā dvāre aṭṭhāsi, sā gantvā caṅkamanasīse gāyi.
rows.english: The elder came out and stood in the door. She went to the end of the walk and sang.

rows.pali: Thero caṅkamanasīsaṃ agamāsi.
rows.english: The elder went to the end of the walk.

rows.pali: Sā sataporise papāte ṭhatvā gāyi.
rows.english: She stood in a chasm a hundred fathoms deep and sang.

rows.pali: Thero paṭinivatti.
rows.english: The elder recoiled.

rows.pali: Atha naṃ sā vegenāgantvā gahetvā "mayā, bhante, na eko na dve tumhādisā khāditā"ti āha.
rows.english: Then she suddenly grabbed him saying, “Venerable sir, it is not just one or two of the likes of you I have eaten. ”

rows.pali: Kalyāṇamittānaṃ alābhoti yattha na sakkā hoti ācariyaṃ vā ācariyasamaṃ vā upajjhāyaṃ vā upajjhāyasamaṃ vā kalyāṇamittaṃ laddhuṃ.
rows.english: 18.18. Lack of good friends: where it is not possible to find a good friend as a teacher or the equivalent of a teacher or a preceptor or the equivalent of a preceptor,

rows.pali: Tattha so kalyāṇamittānaṃ alābho mahādosoyevāti imesaṃ aṭṭhārasannaṃ dosānaṃ aññatarena samannāgato ananurūpoti veditabbo.
rows.english: the lack of good friends there is a serious fault. One that has any of those eighteen faults should be understood as unfavourable.

rows.pali: Vuttampi cetaṃ aṭṭhakathāsu –
rows.english: And this is said in the commentaries:

rows.pali: 53.Yo pana gocaragāmato nātidūranāccāsannatādīhi pañcahaṅgehi samannāgato, ayaṃ anurūpo nāma.
rows.english: 19.One that has the five factors beginning with “not too far from and not too near to” the alms resort is called favourable.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ bhagavatā – "kathañca, bhikkhave, senāsanaṃ pañcaṅgasamannāgataṃ hoti?
rows.english: For this is said by the Blessed One: “And how has a lodging five factors, bhikkhus?

rows.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, senāsanaṃ nātidūraṃ hoti nāccāsannaṃ gamanāgamanasampannaṃ, divā appākiṇṇaṃ rattiṃ appasaddaṃ appanigghosaṃ, appaḍaṃsamakasavātātapasarīsapasamphassaṃ, tasmiṃ kho pana senāsane viharantassa appakasireneva uppajjanti cīvarapiṇḍapātasenāsanagilānapaccayabhesajjaparikkhārā.
rows.english: Here, bhikkhus, (1) a lodging is not too far, not too near, and has a path for going and coming. (2) It is little frequented by day with little sound and few voices by night. (3) There is little contact with gadflies, flies, wind, burning [sun] and creeping things. (4) One who lives in that lodging easily obtains robes, alms food, lodging, and the requisite of medicine as cure for the sick.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ kho pana senāsane therā bhikkhū viharanti bahussutā āgatāgamā dhammadharā vinayadharā mātikādharā, te kālena kālaṃ upasaṅkamitvā paripucchati paripañhati 'idaṃ, bhante, kathaṃ imassa ko attho'ti, tassa te āyasmanto avivaṭañceva vivaranti, anuttānīkatañca uttānīkaronti, anekavihitesu ca kaṅkhaṭṭhāniyesu dhammesu kaṅkhaṃ paṭivinodenti.
rows.english: (5) In that lodging there are elder bhikkhus living who are learned, versed in the scriptures, observers of the Dhamma, observers of the Vinaya, observers of the Codes, and when from time to time one asks them questions, ‘How is this, venerable sir? What is the meaning of this? ’ then those venerable ones reveal the unrevealed, explain the unexplained, and remove doubt about the many things that raise doubts.

rows.pali: Evaṃ kho, bhikkhave, senāsanaṃ pañcaṅgasamannāgataṃ hotī"ti (a. ni. 10.11).
rows.english: This, bhikkhus, is how a lodging has five factors”(A V 15).

rows.pali: Ayaṃ "samādhibhāvanāya ananurūpaṃ vihāraṃ pahāya anurūpe vihāre viharantenā"ti ettha vitthāro.
rows.english: These are the details for the clause, “After that he should avoid a monastery unfavourable to the development of concentration and go to live in one that is favourable” (III.28).

rows.pali: 54.Khuddakapalibodhupacchedaṃ katvāti evaṃ patirūpe vihāre viharantena yepissa te honti khuddakapalibodhā, tepi upacchinditabbā.
rows.english: 20. Then he should sever the lesser impediments (III.28): one living in such a favourable monastery should sever any minor impediments that he may still have,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ, dīghāni kesanakhalomāni chinditabbāni.
rows.english: that is to say, long head hair, nails, and body hair should be cut,

rows.pali: Jiṇṇacīvaresu daḷhīkammaṃ vā tunnakammaṃ vā kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: mending and patching of old robes should be done,

rows.pali: Kiliṭṭhāni vā rajitabbāni.
rows.english: or those that are soiled should be dyed.

rows.pali: Sace patte malaṃ hoti, patto pacitabbo.
rows.english: If there is a stain on the bowl, the bowl should be baked.

rows.pali: Mañcapīṭhādīni sodhetabbānīti.
rows.english: The bed, chair, etc., should be cleaned up.

rows.pali: "Ayaṃ khuddakapalibodhupacchedaṃ katvā"ti ettha vitthāro.
rows.english: These are the details for the clause, “Then he should sever the lesser impediments. ”

rows.pali: 55.Idāni sabbaṃ bhāvanāvidhānaṃ aparihāpentena bhāvetabboti ettha ayaṃ pathavīkasiṇaṃ ādiṃ katvā sabbakammaṭṭhānavasena vitthārakathā hoti.
rows.english: 21. Now, with the clause, And not overlook any of the directions for development (III.28), the time has come for the detailed exposition of all meditation subjects, starting with the earth kasiṇa.

rows.pali: Evaṃ upacchinnakhuddakapalibodhena hi bhikkhunā pacchābhattaṃ piṇḍapātapaṭikkantena bhattasammadaṃ paṭivinodetvā pavivitte okāse sukhanisinnena katāya vā akatāya vā pathaviyā nimittaṃ gaṇhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: [THE EARTH KASIṆA] When a bhikkhu has thus severed the lesser impediments, then, on his return from his alms round after his meal and after he has got rid of drowsiness due to the meal, he should sit down comfortably in a secluded place and apprehend the sign in earth that is either made up or not made up.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ –
rows.english: 22. For this is said:4

rows.pali: Tattha yena atītabhavepi sāsane vā isipabbajjāya vā pabbajitvā pathavīkasiṇe catukkapañcakajjhānāni nibbattitapubbāni, evarūpassa puññavato upanissayasampannassa akatāya pathaviyā kasitaṭṭhāne vā khalamaṇḍale vā nimittaṃ uppajjati, mallakattherassa viya.
rows.english: 23. Herein, when in a previous becoming a man has gone forth into homelessness in the Dispensation or [outside it] with the rishis’ going forth and has already produced the jhāna tetrad or pentad on the earth kasiṇa, and so has such merit and the support [of past practice of jhāna] as well, then the sign arises in him on earth that is not made up, that is to say, on a ploughed area or on a threshing floor, as in the Elder Mallaka’s case.

rows.pali: Tassa kirāyasmato kasitaṭṭhānaṃ olokentassa taṃṭhānappamāṇameva nimittaṃ udapādi.
rows.english: It seems that while that venerable one was looking at a ploughed area the sign arose in him the size of that area.

rows.pali: So taṃ vaḍḍhetvā pañcakajjhānāni nibbattetvā jhānapadaṭṭhānaṃ vipassanaṃ paṭṭhapetvā arahattaṃ pāpuṇi.
rows.english: He extended it and attained the jhāna pentad. Then by establishing insight with the jhāna as the basis for it, he reached Arahantship.

rows.pali: Yo panevaṃ akatādhikāro hoti, tena ācariyasantike uggahitakammaṭṭhānavidhānaṃ avirādhetvā cattāro kasiṇadose pariharantena kasiṇaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: [MAKING AN EARTH KASIṆA] 24. But when a man has had no such previous practice, he should make a kasiṇa, guarding against the four faults of a kasiṇa and not overlooking any of the directions for the meditation subject learnt from the teacher.

rows.pali: Nīlapītalohitaodātasambhedavasena hi cattāro pathavīkasiṇadosā.
rows.english: Now, the four faults of the earth kasiṇa are due to the intrusion of blue, yellow, red or white.

rows.pali: Tasmā nīlādivaṇṇaṃ mattikaṃ aggahetvā gaṅgāvahe mattikāsadisāya aruṇavaṇṇāya mattikāya kasiṇaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: So instead of using clay of such colours, he should make the kasiṇa of clay like that in the stream of the Gangā,5 which is the colour of the dawn.

rows.pali: Tañca kho vihāramajjhe sāmaṇerādīnaṃ sañcaraṇaṭṭhāne na kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: And he should make it not in the middle of the monastery in a place where novices, etc., are about

rows.pali: Vihārapaccante pana paṭicchannaṭṭhāne pabbhāre vā paṇṇasālāya vā saṃhārimaṃ vā tatraṭṭhakaṃ vā kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: but on the confines of the monastery in a screened place, either under an overhanging rock or in a leaf hut. He can make it either portable or as a fixture.

rows.pali: Tatra saṃhārimaṃ catūsu daṇḍakesu pilotikaṃ vā cammaṃ vā kaṭasārakaṃ vā bandhitvā tattha apanītatiṇamūlasakkharakathalikāya sumadditāya mattikāya vuttappamāṇaṃ vaṭṭaṃ limpetvā kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: 25.Of these, a portable one should be made by tying rags of leather or matting onto four sticks and smearing thereon a disk of the size already mentioned, using clay picked clean of grass, roots, gravel, and sand, and well kneaded.

rows.pali: Taṃ parikammakāle bhūmiyaṃ attharitvā oloketabbaṃ.
rows.english: At the time of the preliminary work it should be laid on the ground and looked at.

rows.pali: Tatraṭṭhakaṃ bhūmiyaṃ padumakaṇṇikākārena khāṇuke ākoṭetvā vallīhi vinandhitvā kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: A fixture should be made by knocking stakes into the ground in the form of a lotus calyx, lacing them over with creepers.

rows.pali: Yadi sā mattikā nappahoti, adho aññaṃ pakkhipitvā uparibhāge suparisodhitāya aruṇavaṇṇāya mattikāya vidatthicaturaṅgulavitthāraṃ vaṭṭaṃ kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: If the clay is insufficient, then other clay should be put underneath and a disk a span and four fingers across made on top of that with the quite pure dawn-coloured clay.

rows.pali: Etadeva hi pamāṇaṃ sandhāya "suppamattaṃ vā sarāvamattaṃ vā"ti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: For it was with reference only to measurement that it was said above either the size of a bushel or the size of a saucer (§22).

rows.pali: "Sāntake no anantake"tiādi panassa paricchedatthāya vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But that is bounded, not unbounded was said to show its delimitedness.

rows.pali: 56.Tasmā evaṃ vuttappamāṇaparicchedaṃ katvā rukkhapāṇikā visabhāgavaṇṇaṃ samuṭṭhapeti.
rows.english: 26. So, having thus made it delimited and of the size prescribed, he should scrape it down with a stone trowel—a wooden trowel turns it a bad colour,

rows.pali: Tasmā taṃ aggahetvā pāsāṇapāṇikāya ghaṃsetvā samaṃ bherītalasadisaṃ katvā taṃ ṭhānaṃ sammajjitvā nhatvā āgantvā kasiṇamaṇḍalato aḍḍhateyyahatthantare padese paññatte vidatthicaturaṅgulapādake suatthate pīṭhe nisīditabbaṃ.
rows.english: so that should not be employed—and make it as even as the surface of a drum. Then he should sweep the place out and have a bath. On his return he should seat himself on a well-covered chair with legs a span and four fingers high, prepared in a place that is two and a half cubits [that is, two and a half times elbow to finger-tip] from the kasiṇa disk.

rows.pali: Tato dūratare nisinnassa hi kasiṇaṃ na upaṭṭhāti, āsannatare kasiṇadosā paññāyanti.
rows.english: For the kasiṇa does not appear plainly to him if he sits further off than that; and if he sits nearer than that, faults in the kasiṇa appear.

rows.pali: Uccatare nisinnena gīvaṃ onamitvā oloketabbaṃ hoti, nīcatare jaṇṇukāni rujanti.
rows.english: If he sits higher up, he has to look at it with his neck bent; and if he sits lower down, his knees ache.

rows.pali: Tasmā vuttanayeneva nisīditvā "appassādā kāmā"tiādinā nayena kāmesu ādīnavaṃ paccavekkhitvā kāmanissaraṇe sabbadukkhasamatikkamupāyabhūte nekkhamme jātābhilāsena buddhadhammasaṅghaguṇānussaraṇena pītipāmojjaṃ janayitvā "ayaṃ dāni sā sabbabuddha paccekabuddha ariyasāvakehi paṭipannā nekkhammapaṭipadā"ti paṭipattiyā sañjātagāravena "addhā imāya paṭipadāya pavivekasukharasassa bhāgī bhavissāmī"ti ussāhaṃ janayitvā samena ākārena cakkhūni ummīletvā nimittaṃ gaṇhantena bhāvetabbaṃ.
rows.english: [STARTING CONTEMPLATION] 27.So, after seating himself in the way stated, he should review the dangers in sense desires in the way beginning, “Sense desires give little enjoyment” (M I 91) and arouse longing for the escape from sense desires, for the renunciation that is the means to the surmounting of all suffering. He should next arouse joy of happiness by recollecting the special qualities of the Buddha, the Dhamma, and the Sangha; then awe by thinking, “Now, this is the way of renunciation entered upon by all Buddhas, Paccekabuddhas and noble disciples”; and then eagerness by thinking, “In this way I shall surely come to know the taste of the bliss of seclusion.” [125] After that he should open his eyes moderately, apprehend the sign, and so proceed to develop it. 6

rows.pali: Atiummīlayato hi cakkhu kilamati, maṇḍalañca ativibhūtaṃ hoti, tenassa nimittaṃ nuppajjati.
rows.english: 28.If he opens his eyes too wide, they get fatigued and the disk becomes too obvious, which prevents the sign becoming apparent to him.

rows.pali: Atimandaṃ ummīlayato maṇḍalamavibhūtaṃ hoti, cittañca līnaṃ hoti, evampi nimittaṃ nuppajjati.
rows.english: If he opens them too little, the disk is not obvious enough, and his mind becomes drowsy, which also prevents the sign becoming apparent to him.

rows.pali: Tasmā ādāsatale mukhanimittadassinā viya samenākārena cakkhūni ummīletvā nimittaṃ gaṇhantena bhāvetabbaṃ, na vaṇṇo paccavekkhitabbo, na lakkhaṇaṃ manasikātabbaṃ.
rows.english: So he should develop it by apprehending the sign (nimitta), keeping his eyes open moderately, as if he were seeing the reflection of his face (mukha-nimitta) on the surface of a looking- glass. 7 29.The colour should not be reviewed. The characteristic should not be given attention.8

rows.pali: Apica vaṇṇaṃ amuñcitvā nissayasavaṇṇaṃ katvā ussadavasena paṇṇattidhamme cittaṃ paṭṭhapetvā manasi kātabbaṃ.
rows.english: But rather, while not ignoring the colour, attention should be given by setting the mind on the [name] concept as the most outstanding mental datum, relegating the colour to the position of a property of its physical support.

rows.pali: Pathavī mahī, medinī, bhūmi, vasudhā, vasundharātiādīsu pathavīnāmesu yamicchati, yadassa saññānukūlaṃ hoti, taṃ vattabbaṃ.
rows.english: That [conceptual state] can be called by anyone he likes among the names for earth (pathavī) such as “earth” (pathavī), “the Great One” (mahī), “the Friendly One” (medinī), “ground” (bhūmi), “the Provider of Wealth” (vasudhā), “the Bearer of Wealth” (vasudharā), etc., whichever suits his manner of perception.
